video_title: Cockatoo And Baby Boy Do Everything Together | The Dodo Soulmates
video_description: Instant besties Keep up with Grietje, Marni, and Rémi on Instagram: http://thedo.do/marnithecockatoo. Introducing Dodo swag!
text_content: Here on my farm here in Australia. I've had the same wild sulphur crested cockatoo visiting me for over 35 years. It was a young bird when I was young and I used to put out sunflower seeds and water for it and other birds. I would sit on the bottom step of my homestead and hold my hand out with seeds for them. After many tries the young cockatoo came and ate out of my hand. And from then on, it got more and more comfortable with me and eventually it would sit on my arm and eat the sunflower seeds. It would stay around the homestead yard most of the day  either sleeping in a tree, swimming in the large birdbath or flying around. I eventually taught it to talk and say hello. For a wild animal its more like a pet. I don't feed it every day but he (in sure it's a male) comes to visit and is very placed and gentle and doesn't bite.  He plays around with my dogs and often comes with his entire family.  I'm very lucky to have a wild animal stay around for so long. 😊

text_content: Worlds best mom. Instead of doing what so many parents do by rehoming animals because they are too lazy to take the time to make the animals fit in with the new change of the baby, she includes her pet with everything involving her baby. I love everything about this!

video_title: Cockatoo And Baby Boy Do Everything Together | The Dodo Soulmates
video_description: Instant besties Keep up with Grietje, Marni, and Rémi on Instagram: http://thedo.do/marnithecockatoo. Introducing Dodo swag!
text_content:
Can I just say... this parent knows what she's doing. Like any pet and kid, it's a hit/miss situation whether the kid and the pet get along or not, birds especially. I absolutely love how this mom has helped strengthen the bond between the cockatoo and the baby. I think the thing I love most about this was that the mom got toys of the bird to help the kid to not be scared of him. Birds with those kinds of beaks and of that kind of size can scare some people, so I'm really happy that that mom was able to make the bird seem like a friend in that way. And I'm really happy that she's supervising them when together, that way they won't hurt the other. She knows what she's doing. 

I also loved that she knew to dote on both the baby and bird equally. It's SO imperative that this happens. They're both kids in a way and both deserve that love and affection. 

I truly hope that the kid and the cockatoo will stay buddies for many years to come. Great job, Mom!

video_title: Cockatoo And Baby Boy Do Everything Together | The Dodo Soulmates
video_description: Instant besties Keep up with Grietje, Marni, and Rémi on Instagram: http://thedo.do/marnithecockatoo. Introducing Dodo swag!
text_content: Birds are so smart. They are just like children themselves so I can see why jealousy or even a brotherly love is possible. What a special friendship that will be a awesome teaching tool for the child as well about respect and nurturing of all wild life whether in home or in wild. I find kids who spend allot of time caring for animals as kids, have huge hearts as they grow up in this very cruel cold world. So important to the soul to be a animal lover and admirer, helps us deal with everyday life but also teaches how better to interact with people.

text_content: What a wonderful and wise mother, teaching her child kindness and respect for a fellow creature.  I loved that she referred to Marni as Remi's brother and was envisioning them spending their lives together.  It gives me great hope to see people becoming increasingly aware that we are not superior to any other creature and that we must protect and cherish all life and teach our children this fundamental lesson.  I find animal cruelty (any cruelty) unbearable and pray for the shift in our collective consciousness that will finally take us from this psychopathic age into the empathetic, operating at the highest vibrational level which is love.

text_content: I live in Australia and these birds are the biggest trouble makers. Some towns will put bird spikes up to stop cockatoos from landing in places where they chew things up and these guys are so smart and mischievous that they will rip the spikes out designed specifically to stop them. Today I have myself a mix with seeds nuts and fruit and I intend to find a flock and have them climb over me and my kids to get it. These guys are absolute characters both as pets and in wild flocks across Australia.

text_content:
As a parrot owner of 27 years - THANK YOU for giving the negative points to owning a parrot.  Everyone shows the cutesy, cuddly parts of life with a parrot, but rarely do they give the warnings.  I studied them for a year before I decided on a Congo African Grey.  I knew I would get bitten.  I knew they required time and attention.  I have many scars on my fingers, and one or two on my lips.  We love our boys, but we also understand them.  You HAVE to be able to read body language.  Greys are not as attention seeking a 'toos are, but they can be very aggressive during mating season - spring & fall.  They do not allow deviation from a set schedule, and they need a schedule.
Our orange-winged Amazon is very sweet and gentle.  The only time he bites is when there is a child in the house.  When a child enters, he no longer wants much to do with me.  He wants to cuddle with the child.  Otherwise he is patient, he is sweet - but when he screams - he makes your eardrums shake.  And he screams fairly often.
I really appreciate you putting it out there so people know what they are getting into.

He would be sweet and funny in a room full of men and see a woman out the window and his eyes would dilate in and out and his voice would sound satanic and then he would flip out and attack the closest person to him.  He trapped my male friend in a bedroom because he saw a women knock on the front door and we had to "rescue" my friend.  We found a home for him with a gay couple who knew what he was like and they still have him to this day - 40 years later.

text_content: I have two sun conures, a yellow naped amazon,  and a male umbrella cockatoo. Figured if I could handle sun conure noise, I'd be find with a cockatoo. Man, was I wrong! Basically, it's like living with a 3yr old who's bi-polar . My other birds aren't nearly as needy. Thought I did my due diligence before getting him, but there's really not many people willing to tell the truth.  Everything you said was dead on, down to having to use locks on his cage. Took two locks (the one with keys) to seal the nesting box entrance because he broke the bolts holding it together, and his cage is 60 x 60!  Honestly, they shouldn't be pets. They're not domesticated like dogs and cats. Love my little guy to death, and I have no intention of placing him in yet another home.......but if I knew what I was really in for before getting him, I wouldn't have done it..... that's the truth. Having said that, I've never laughed so hard at a birds antics......but that's maybe 5% of the time. The rest is not always so easy. I'd love to know how you got him not to scream so much. My guy is good as long as he can see me. Can't even go to the bathroom without him screaming the house down. Good thing we live on 40 acres on top of a mountain. 😂

text_content:
1.  They're loud.  Some owners use gun range ear sets to cancel out the noise.  It's not enough to lighten the sound when the bird screams on days when you have a headache, want to get away from it.  The sound is meant to travel for over 10 miles in the wild.   Very loud.  You have neighbors?   Live in an apartment?  Your landlord and neighbors will hate you.  There will be days YOU will hate the noise too.
2.  Expensive.   A bird this size needs a flight cage.   A cage no smaller than a king size bed.  You have room for that?   They also need regular toe and beak grooming.  If you're not capable of doing it, can you afford to take the bird to a vet and get it done once a month at least?  Beak and toes also need coconut oil massages, regularly.  You feel like doing that for 80 years?   They live up to 80 years.   Who takes care of the bird if you die suddenly?   Got plans for that?  And they need a good diet.   Chewing is a must to keep beak in good condition.  They MUST chew.  So supplying them with toys or hard nuts is not optional, it's a must-have.  Can you afford their diet?  Or are you going to be a bad owner, that gives them the bare minimum for them to survive?   Too lazy to do fresh fruit and vegetables for them daily?  Don't get a bird.  And don't think you can skip the cage.  They're a 2 year old that never grows up.   Ever.  They need a time out cage.  It's their chill out zone.
3.  Time consuming.  You MUST read at least a pile of bird training books and behavior books BEFORE adopting a bird so you know the signs.  Training the bird takes time.  And has to be done a few minutes every day.   This bird depends on you.   You need to KNOW what you will do to this bird if you abandon it, go off on vacation, leave the bird behind, what medical problems you will be inflicting on your bird.  Plucking is only the most common you'll read about.  You need to read a pile of books BEFORE YOU ADOPT A BIRD so you can also be wise to medical problems.  You need to recognize medical issues long before they're obvious.   A bird can go south quickly.   These are not birds you can abandon, no large bird is.  And you're shit if you adopt one and abandon it.

4.  Bored bird = living terror!   Are you smarter than a 5th grader?  If not, don't get a bird.  Need to be smarter than the bird in order to keep it mentally stimulated and out of trouble.   Need to be emotionally mature enough to be able to handle a 2 year old tossing a tantrum without loosing your cool.  Got a temper?  Don't get a bird.
5.  KNOW the people you're getting the bird from.   Are they good people?   Are you accidentally supporting bird breeders that are flooding the market for a profit, not caring what happens to the babies?  It's the same thing as a puppymill.   Are you backing that?  Now days you have to be careful of even adoption agencies for these birds.   Even some of them are corrupt.   Do you know?  Done your homework?  I won't do it for you.   That,  you research yourself.    In order for it to stop, people need to get wise to it.  And they won't get wise unless they do the leg work.

text_content: I have one and i regret getting one everyday, my family decided to get African gray so we went to a pet shop and bought a new born African gray, the bird turned out to be disabled and it had heart problems so the shop gave us our money back. They offered us a baby Umbrella cockatoo instead and we took it. The cockatoo was a female and she was so big compared to regular cockatoos. We didn’t know how bad cockatoos were before we bought them we just had conures before. My pet cockatoo is 7 now and she just has so many issues. She gets super sexual with us so we cant touch her below the neck, we learned that the hard way after she laid 4 eggs between the ages of 4-6💀 terrible ik. We trained her to stay on her cage with our other bird which is a nanday conure. They get alone just fine and rarely fight but when they do she always does something horrible like once she bite a hole in the other birds beak. She has never not once bit a human before though. She so loving and sweet but also a demon at the same time. She has gotten out and ruined tv remotes and cords before. She also has butt problems because she prolapse herself whenever she feels like she doesn’t have enough attention. We had to result into spraying her with water because she pushes her organ out if she doesnt get sprayed. She has been doing her butt thing for years now and she never cleans her butt because she started at a young age. Not her fault at all by the way she has behavior issues because we gave her too much attention. I was like 10 when we got her and I’m 17 now. Lover her to death but she would be better off in the wild 😂. She’s not aggressive at all and she is well trained, if we say go up she runs up her cage, if we say get in the cage she runs right in.  We fixed her sexual issue by not letting her on the couch with us. She has to stay on the cage, once in a whole we’ll let her sit on our knee on the couch but other then that she stays on her cage. She chose my father from day one because he never feared her at all and he is also the person she fears the most. She loves my dad but also fears my dad so much, we don’t abuse her at all btw she just fears my dad because he has a big beard and a deep voice and he’s very tall. She has eacaped the cage so many times but recently she has been escaping her cage and staying on top of her cage with the other bird which is progress 😂. She’s just starting to become better because we try to not cuddle her or anything and she talks sooooo much. She got her butt problem original from a stomach illness btw, it started from that and we gave her attention when she would push her stuff out, now she thinks that’s like the bell that get’s everyone’s attention. She is a handful and i regret wanting her lol. (Edit) btw i just wanted to mention she has a huge cage with a nice special structure on top where she chills for most of the day and it’s in the living room so she sees us all day and she gets attention all the time.

text_content: As a carer for a cockatoo I found your video so true,  one thing i will mention that I have found especially with my Greater crested sulphur cockatoo called Buster but also with other species of cockatoos that I have cared for, is how he will pretend to like a visitor,  No matter how often I warn my visitors not to trust his sweet ways, He will act so sweet and charming just to lure them closer so he can bite them, as you know his bite can be nasty.  I keep him caged when I have Visitors but he will act so nice and loving that he has bitten five visitors.  He is so intelligent  you can see his thought process but Too's can move there head so fast from a head bowed down to bite the finger is so fast and quicker than a humans reaction.  I have never been bit by a Cockatoo and I can do anything with Buster but he is a one person bird.  I have over 50 birds in my home and most flying free,  His cage was for a green winged macaw but the strength in his beak has bent some of the bars and removed one,  How ever He gets on with the other flock members providing they are not cockatoos or eclectus parrots and most nights two of my budgies and a pair of zebra finches will get through the bars to sleep next to him, also three of my ringnecks and two of my amazons will sleep on top of his cage at night.   Thank you for making this video and for the love and understanding of your birds,

text_content: You’re spot on with your assessment and advice. If I didn’t already have a Parrot and somewhat know their behavior I would be completely lost with my new Umbrella Cockotoo. My friend passed from cancer and asked me to give her animals a home. It’s been 6 months and he is finally settling in and beginning to know me better and me him. I’ve also learned a lot about this parrot by researching and watching videos like yours. My yellow nape Amazon is an Angel compared to this one, he is almost 35 years old, he was my brothers, he’s been with me for 31 years! Cesar has gone through 3 homes, and I’m hoping we can live together, but again, we’re learning, me him and vice versa. I hate caged birds, they should be free not caged up and then subject to being in captivity. Thank you for the video. I don’t think an inexperienced person should adopt one of these birds.

video_title: sisu #birds #cockatoos #cockatoobird #cockatoo #sisu #pet #funny #parrot #bird #amazon
text_content: I've had a cockatoo and I've had a blue front Amazon.  I would never let them be close to each other. Cockatoos are extremely intelligent birds. They have the emotional intelligence of a 5-year-old. Their  jealousy drives them to harm or kill their rival which could be a guinea pig or a hamster, or a cat or a dog.  They will go after them if they think that you're not giving them enough attention and love. I ended up giving our cockatoo to a vet lab technician who had always wanted a cockatoo and she had no other animals so it's a perfect home for him.  So this cockatoo is not showing dominance only, he is showing that he will attack and he will harm that bird.  The cockatoo I had, named Kazoo,  had to be pulled off of my African Grey, named Pepper, and he had punctured her cheek and her wing.  And that was not the first incident I had with a cockatoo he went after my darling little Harley bird who is what we call a pocket parrot.  He hurt Harley so badly in his wing we had to amputate the wing as it was destroyed. Harley was a feisty little parrot and he climbed off of his cage one day and marched over to Kazoos cage because he was going to finish what Kazoo had started and I'm so glad I was there to scoop him up and lock him back in his cage or he would have been killed that day.  Something I find very appalling on the internet with animals is people get animals they don't understand they don't know anything about their breed or what the needs are for that individual animal.  I handled Birds since I was 10 years old and I'm in my 70s now and I am appalled at how little people understand are know about birds they are highly intelligent more so than a dog is believe it or not.

text_content: I have had 8 birds (2 cockatoos, 3 Amazon parrots, and 3 macaws) over the years. All but one were rescues. I’ve lost three to cancer. My oldest was with me 36 years. They are so precious and so many are in need of rescue. Thank you for raising awareness for these beautiful creatures.

text_content: I have had my cockatoo (Socks) for 3 years and ever since that first night I have NEVER spent a night away from him. No more vacations, no more nights out with friends or weekends away. He is the first thing I give attention to when I wake in the morning, when I arrive home from work whether I am hungry, tired or need a bathroom break. He is the last thing I attend to before I go to bed and many times I just give in and allow him to sleep on the pillow next to me. I can be honest and say that sometimes it can be overwhelming but I know he is my responsibility. I am his human and he is emotionally attached to me. I am 57 years old and it is a fact that he will very likely out live me and I have made provisions for that in my will. Before getting him I was told that cockatoos love hard but I did not fully comprehend this. If you are not capable of giving these beautiful beings your love and attention then please do not get one. They are not pets, they are companions and a part of your family.

Birds can absolutely sense what is going on with their humans. While my husband was dying under hospice care in our home, Squinty, our cockatiel, sat on his chest. He only moved when I needed to care for my husband. For several days, Squinty stayed low on his chest, quietly keeping watch. After my husband passed, Squinty became depressed. He stayed in his cage in the corner and wouldn’t come out.
Birds just know.

It's amazing how smart birds can be. I've had several beautiful birds that bonded with me. Of my flock of four laying hens one even recognized I was hurt when I strained my back repairing a fence after a tornado. As I was laying on my back until the pain subsided she came to me, stroked my side with her foot, climbed on my chest, and nestled in my armpit to comfort me. 

A beautiful Great Snowy Egret waits in sight of the park gate where me, and my dog enter on his daily long walk. On my bicycle with the dog running beside me she'll fly, sometimes right next to my shoulder, with us. 

A Blue Heron attracted to eat the fish drawn to the bread when I feed the Canadian Geese, and Mallard Ducks bonded with me. Feeding right by my knee for years she twice hopped to me, and gave me a fish two days in a row.

This story should also serve as an incentive to demand that _all_ "animal breeders" be recognized as the evil @$$holes they truly are.

Animal "breeding" should be *_harshly_* regulated, if even permitted at all, regardless of which species is  held captive. 

They should all be subjected to frequent unnanounced checks by veterinarians and law enforcement to ensure they can't do to even one more bird or other animal what was done to Victoria the Cockatoo.

If any "breeder" is found to be abusing, neglecting, and forcing any animal to subsist in suboptimal conditions, the animals should be immediately seized and evacuated to safety, and the captor charged with every single violation and ruthlessly prosecuted. 

And one such epidode should permanently preclude them from EVER having any more animals in their clutches ever again, either. 

*I do not care one iota* about the "Oh, poor me, it's my livelihood!" whining as an excuse to abuse, torture, deprive, and neglect other species held as *_slaves,_* forced to live in misery each and every day because some lazy, greedy, and reckless @$$hole is using animal misery to make money.

Screw that, and screw them. 

Those types deserve to suffer the way they make other beings suffer, and I don't care who's feelings get hurt reading this, either. 

If you make your money on the backs of captive slaves, then _you deserve to suffer equal consequences for your crimes._

Victoria's life was significantly shortened due the abuse and torture she suffered. 

_There is no excuse that will ever suffice,_ no second chances deserved, no mercy extended, and no f*cks given about @$$holes who treat animals the way Victoria was treated. 

I just can't care about anyone who whines about how they they are "overegulated" by authorities when they are greedy, abusive pieces of 💩!

text_content:
The first parrots I ever fell in love with were a hyacinth named Puni and a scarlet named Celeste. They were such sweet birds. We were buddies when I was lucky enough to hang out with them, and I've been wanting one since I met them.

Saying that, their person was a VERY experienced parrot owner whose life revolved around his birds. Seeing how much care and time they required helped me understand that no matter how much I loved them, it's hard to give them a good life, and it's cruel to take one on if you're not prepared. They're less like dogs or cats, and more like having a flying, screaming toddler with bolt cutters attached to their faces.

I have a blue and gold macaw and I have a theory about why they are called blue and gold macaws. Most of their bigger feathers are blue, black, purple, yellow and on the back side: gold.  And this isn’t very noticeable when you see blue and gold macaw on videos and pictures, but when you see the shedded flight feathers you will notice it. Those feathers are perfectly described by «blue and gold».

Also, I do disagree with you, I think the blue snd golds belly and underside areas are more 50% yellow and 50% orange. It’s perfectly in between the two, so it’s always difficult to decide if it should be called yellow or orange.

text_content:
Thank you for doing this. Educating people about not impulse buying is so important because so many kids can watch things and get this wistful image of the perfect pet in a perfect world.

I know I have a personal dream to one day be able to live a relatively good life that's perfect for raising a Mollucan Cockatoo. I've done a lot of research and I know full well what it takes to take care of even the simplest of parrots - and trust me. There is no simple parrot.

You have to devote a lot of time, money, and love into them. Most, if not all, parrots are as intelligent as a 3 year old child. A toddler! And they live upwards of 20 to 80 years on average, depending on size and species. They're lifetime commitments. You'll literally be raising a toddler for a majority of your living life. While that can be rewarding for those who know what they're up against and are prepared for that, then they're perfect additions to the family. But you should never impulse buy any animal. Ever. You should always do your research and prepare, be it a mouse, a dog, a parrot, or a horse. Always make sure you can actually PROVIDE before you OWN.

video_description: Don't buy a macaw!! Unless”, is a video we felt we had to make which shows some of the real things about owning a macaw or ...
text_content:
As a owner of a parrot for 12 years:
1.) Macaws are the LOUDEST parrots in the rainforest. So they will scream EXTREMELY LOUD when they play, want attention, are upset, when they want your food, ect.
2.) Their intelligence is the equivalent to a human toddler. They need as much attention, love, & play as a human toddler. If you don't give them this time they will have significant mental health & physical healthy problems.
3.) They go through puberty just like humans. Once the reach puberty (in their early teens) their personality changes. They will become more aggressive, jealous if u hangout with anyone else, and randomly biting. 
4.) They poop on EVERYTHING constantly. They poop every 15 minutes so you have to be ok with them pooping on ur furniture, nice house items, & with deep cleaning the cage every couple of days.
5.) Vet bills are EXTREMELY EXPENSIVE. The average vet bill for me was $400. When she became sick it was around $1,000 each vet visit for mediations & tests.
6.) Their average lifespan in captivity is the human life span. If something happens to you or if u adopt a young macaw they will out live you. They have SEVERE PTSD if they have to switch owners because of the deep emotional connection. This can lead to self mutilation.
7.) They love only 1 human at a time. They will be jealous & hate any other humans you are close to.

text_content: A while ago my mom impulse bought a blue and gold from someone. When we first got it I was probably around 9-10. He’s almost the same age as me. I’m 14 now and until recently I wanted nothing to do with him. But my parents started talking about rehoming him and that’s when I realized I actually like him a lot. My parents have done nothing to train him or even spent much time with him so he screams and bites. My parents always start yelling at him when he screams and to calm him down all I have to do is go up and talk to him. This video has inspired me to help him out and get him to become friendly. I’m going to start working with him on a daily basis. I’ve noticed that he’s always been very interested and curious about me. I feel like I can make his life a lot better.

text_content: I am a disabled combat vet,  most of my buddies are married with kids and have jobs, other responsibilities. So I do consulting work from home. Basically, I have all the time in the world. Not married. No kids. When I was a kid, I went on vacation to Colombia. They had two dogs, a cat, two, parrots, and a blue and yellow macaw. The moment I saw it sitting on his perch outside, that was it. Not a day went by were we did not see each other. I fed her, I cuddled her, showered her. The other green parrots were so jealous, even when i played with them too, they just could not be on my shoulders at the same time. They went at it like an avian UFC. I spoiled the heck out of her, which was a big mistake. A few days before I left, the owners, rightfully, put her in an island next to the hotel to distant me from her. I kid you not. She would call out my name. We knew the owners of the establishment, and sadly she died not long after I left. For anybody thinking about getting one of these beauties they can get incredibly attached to their owners. Thinking about getting one since ill be able to take care of it. text_content:
Im an animal behaviourist who specialises in macaws, and I have to say that for 18 months, an unresearched impulse buy, you are doing great! As to all the things he does that you dont love very much (carpet removal, door pruning, screeching, grabbing, etc) these are things that are easily fixed with help from a parrot behaviourist, in person or via live video on pm, etc. Lessons that you, your partner and Mikey all get to learn together, problem solving where you all have a piece of the puzzle.
I would recommend you finding a macaw behaviorist sooner rather than later, to help you fix the negative issues. MY blue and gold wears a harness and goes everywhere I do, as he is a heart monitor and pre warns before I'm going to collapse, so believe me when I say your Mikey is SMART, and being a little rotter pushing the boundries with you, playing at "dumb birdy" ...and conned you successfully! Welcome to parronthood 😁 (some days your fid is just going to be a d@#&) best thing you can do atm is learn his real capabilities re understanding you both, and what his iq and eq ratings are, then tailor his learning appropriately.
I am worried about your mesh by the way. It doesnt look stainless steel and anything else is poisonous to macaws in particular... It is why most bird rooms have only free standing perches and those hung from the ceiling (hook screwed through a frisbee so it cant be chewed out of ceiling) and all lights and windows covered with perspex or stainless cages, and a security screen door in place of the bedroom door!
Same with most bird toys you buy... licking and chewing at chrome chains kill macaws.

In short, it's basically like having a permanent three year old to take care off.

And I honestly think that is a good way to view these birds, they are highly intelligent and I've heard they can even suffer from lasting mental trauma's if they aren't properly taken care off.

text_content: I'm gonna be honest, I agree with the no clipping. My mother has raised me to believe that owning birds that can fly is cruel, unless you have a space where they can fly. text_content: My parents bought a macaw before they had children. Macaws & children DON'T mix. When I turned 12 I "saved her" & developed a relationship with her. We were insperatable until she passed away 2 years ago. So I took care of her for 12 years. text_content: Wonderful video on how to be a great friend to these awesome birds. I am a board member for Exotic Bird Rescue of Oregon (USA) and am blessed with a loving Catalina Macaw myself. People, please listen very closely to this message. It is wonderfully put. On average, exotic birds are re-homed, more than 5 times before finally finding a 'forever' home. and the effects on them can be horrendous. Everything from self-mutilation (feather plucking and even tearing holes in their own flesh) to nearly un-controllable screaming and aggression. (Nearly...  a lot of work and love can remedy a lot of problems).  Also, find a bird club in your area and get some experience with them. Learning to read body language can save the bird and YOU a lot of blood and tears.  I only have one... small... disagreement, and that is with clipping wings and free flighting. I agree that clipping wings seem a travesty but there are situations where, for the safety of the bird, I would recommend it. Also being part of a rescue I can assure you that even well-trained birds get scared and fly away on occasion... sometimes never to be seen (alive at least) again. We get several calls a week regarding birds that 'have never flown off before' having been scared and are gone....it is heartbreaking. If you do take your bird outside....please PLEASE think about an appropriate flight harness.

I wish it was required to take lessons about the bird you want to adopt or buy before you are allowed to own one. People should learn about the needs of their future companion and also get exposed to the noise and judge if they can handle it. And it should be included in those lessons what a naked bird looks like. One that has plucked itself to the point where it can't grow its feathers back and has to stay naked for the rest of his considerably long life. 
I've been living with one or more pets for almost all my life which is nearly 60 years now. And I got all my animals first and started to read books upon books afterwards. You'd think that I got wise after it happened once or twice, but it happened over and over. The reason is simple. There was a pet in need that the owner wanted to get rid of and I felt I had the space, the time and the level of commitment to take it in. 
I'm glad it never was a parrot because I'm not sure I could have handled that without being the one being unhappy in the relationship. Because once I commit I stick with it especially if the pet bonded with me and relied on me to take proper care. I like birds and I enjoyed living with a "flock" of 6 budgies that spent most of the day on a bird tree in the living room outside their cage. But that's worlds apart from living with a parrot. So I'm gonna stick watching parrots on YouTube enjoy other people loving their birds.

Yep! I actually got my girl free, because the person who had her was having problems with her causing all of her other birds to get into trouble and then sitting back and chilling. Billy had the run of my house, and was actually pretty quiet. And at my house, you'd see the bird chasing the cats, not the cats chasing the bird. BUT....I will say that the small cage has it's use in an emergency. When i had been up north for a bit, and took Billy with me, we had a power outage and ice storm. The house dropped to 20 degrees f at night. We kept her warm at night by putting her in the the small emergency cage in front of the fireplace, put plastic gallons of hot water around it then threw quilts over it. Kept her warm all night, which I couldn't have done otherwise. 
Billy wasn't that destructive either. Even when no one was home (and she still had full run of the house). But she had the cats and a dog to play with and I always left a radio on or TV, so there was always someone around for her. And I agree about the wings. Don't clip.. she came with clipped wings and when she'd try to fly, she'd end up hitting a wall or door. Letting her have her wings back ended that issue. And, towards the end of her life, she became "mommy" to 3 orphaned kittens and an orphaned newborn goat (it was dead winter and I had her in the house so she'd survive). She'd preen them, discipline them, curl up and sleep with them...Basically did everything a mom would do but feed them and was their "mom" for the rest of her life. She ended up dying from a series of strokes. As she was already an adult when I got her, and she was an adult when the other person had gotten her, I had no clue as to her age. She was burried with my dog and potbellied pig, who were her 2 best buddies for a good part of her life!

text_content:
One correction: Macaws live around 30 years in the wild, but can live up to 100 years in captivity. They are wild animals and as such are very difficult pets to care for, and only an experienced exotic bird owner should own one.

They’re incredibly intelligent, friendly, and loyal, but require as much attention and work as a small child, AND they can literally deafen you with their incredibly loud screams, _AND_ are capable of biting through a broomstick with their beaks.

I don’t say this to be mean to macaws, but because I rescue parrots, and strongly believe you ought to require a license to own certain larger species which are sold through the exotic pet trade. Macaws and cockatoos often end up having at least two, three, even four plus owners in their lifetimes because people think it will be like owning a dog, but can’t handle the noise or the biting or the constant care needed, and the poor macaws end up homeless in a pet rescue facility.

*Please do NOT buy a macaw. If you are committed to owning one, ALWAYS rescue a homeless bird instead of buying one!*

text_content:
I grew up in a macaw household. They can live to 100 in captivity, and often outlive their owners. My parents had 3 when I was born, and they re-homed 2 of them because 4 children was too much for them. As a result, I had a scarlet macaw named Kimba in my home until she passed from a stroke when I was about 4. I still remember my mom in tears, holding the colorfulbird in her arms, unable to save her. Not long after, we purchased a B&G macaw from a breeder. I remember walking into the "pet store" in Hollywood, and seeing several pin-feathered birds on a table. That was 1983. The pin-feathered creature we took home that day became my bird brother, and still lives with my dad. He has arteriosclerosis, and must be medicated to prevent/delay a fatal stroke.

They are incredible animals, with the mentality of a human 2y/o for their entire lives. They can be very loving and cuddly, protective and loyal, and super destructive, all in the same day. If you're thinking of adopting a macaw, treat the decision with the same seriousness as the thought of adopting a special-needs human child. It takes that much dedication, and someone else will need to be ready to take on that responsibility when you die.

video_description: Where is ok to pet or scratch a macaw? Or any parrot for that matter. Is it ok to pet a parrot on the wings or on the body?
text_content:
My macaw loooves scritches around the ears and under her chin.  She appreciates massage of the spot where beak grows out from cheeks.

This is hard/embarrassing to admit, but I'll talk about it in hopes it helps someone else understand....

Unfortunately, I'd already had her for several years before she hit puberty and the hormonal behaviors kicked in.  I started researching those behaviors and then I began to understand how wrong it was to pet her back, wings, and tail. This is mating behavior for birds.

I'm sorry I ever petted her like that. I was ignorant. I'd never had a bird before her and I learned bird behavior mostly from her.  

Anyway, even though I stopped petting her back/wings/tail, I'm  
very sorry i ever thought that a bird could be petted likea cat or dog. I didn't understand how inappropriate i was being or how much more difficult it would make her transition to puberty.

I think we'd have a better relationship now (and she'd be happier) if I'd known then how wrong it was.

text_content:
My RB Cockatoo has arthritis and loves her feet massaged. I'll put coconut oil on her feet everyday, and she's learned to "ask" for the oil by lifting up one of her feet when I'm giving her loves (aka scritches on her head).

Speaking of a bird's head, when they are molting, the shafts get pretty sharp. The bird's head is more sensitive than normal during this time. It's a good idea to gently break the shafts apart by rubbing each of them between the thumb and forefinger. Otherwise, the feathers poke them while you're petting them. This is painful and that can result in a bite, too.

text_content:
We just rescued a blue and gold Macaw named Roscoe. We were told he we *roughly* 14 years old, but the people we got him from said they only had him for four years, so they're aren't even sure. 

He had quite an attachment to me very quick, which is crazy because I've never been a bird owner before until I met my Fiancée, who hand raised and sold birds as a teenager and has dealt with them her whole life. 

But he is very territorial and will bark and scream at us if we get too close or hug and kiss on one another. And if I'm not in the room with her, he will snap at her and try to bite, but never does that to me. She hasn't done anything to him, and I haven't done anything special to deserve his undying love and affection. So i was curious if that seems to be typical of them. 

For some experience context for myself; She had a cockatiel when we got together, named Blaiz, and we rescued a blind and arthritic orange winged amazon parrot shortly after we got together, named Peter. Both alive and as well as can be. 
Blaiz is now 15, cranky, old boy, but he belonged to her son, who unfortunately passed away a few months back, so there is 0 chance we get rid of him. 

Peter is 43. He was in a home that didn't care about him. His original owner passed away a few years back from old age, and the lady we got him from didn't want him, nor did she take care of him. His beak was so overgrown that it's now permanently shifted his jaw so his mouth is crooked. And his talons were so overgrown that he couldn't walk on his feet, to which one grew very stiff, and now he can't open it all the way. (Vet said there was nothing we could do about it because it had progressed too far for medicine to be of any use.)

Roscoe is very healthy, aside from the awful diet the last couple had him on, which consisted of peanuts, lots of fried potatoes, and paper. 🙃

We have fixed the diet. He now has a regular diet of fresh fruits and veggies as he should, with a nutriberri blend that we mix plumage food into. And the occasional peanut or pistachio for a treat. 

He's a tricky little guy, but he is a huge sweetheart. He has an attitude, but he is much larger than Peter, so while he has never attacked me, (he snapped at me once, but didn't hurt or even leave a mark, so I'm sure he was just playing) I'm still pretty nervous around him. He let's me pick him up, and pet his head. I haven't tried his face around his eyes yet, so I'm going to try that today and see if he's okay with it. 

I was just wondering if you had any tips for helping him adjust to her? She's always been a huge bird lover, and she was so excited to get Roscoe. He was not very nice to her the first week we had him, and she got very frustrated by him, and actually yelled (her yells aren't very yelly) at him. Blaiz is actually a total asshole because her son didn't really take care of him, and I've seen Blaiz bite her and draw blood and she's never gotten mad enough to yell at him. So I can tell that she's really upset by his behavior, more so because he is so nice to me. I just want to do my best to help him adjust to her, too. 

I'm sorry that was a lot of stuff to say. 😅

video_title: I Wish I Never Knew This

text_content: This is called "preening". When birds grow new feathers, they start to grow "pins", which are little quills with tinny blood vessels. When the feather grew all the way, the blood vessel retracts and the remaining's dry off. Usually birds are able to get rid of most of their pins by themselves but the ones in their tails, head and neck. In the wild, other birds would help them, in captivity, they need help. It's very satisfying and a great way to bond with your birb <3.

video_title: I Wish I Never Knew This
video_description: Source: BViral #shorts.
text_content: PS they’re called pin feathers and they’re really extremely very itchy. They can get many of them at once when they go through a ‘molt’ when the seasons change. In flocks they will ‘preen’ each other to help ease the pain.

video_title: Why People Loves Macaw Parrot So Much
video_description: shorts #macaw #birds #pet #cute #cuteanimals #pets #animal #animals #animalfacts #animalshorts #animaldocumentary #nature ...
text_content:
Please don’t make parrots seem like “easy” pets!! So many parrots are abandoned or neglected because people get them because of videos like this without doing proper research, and they’re such emotional and intelligent animals and deserve better. 

Everyone, please do proper research before getting a parrot! They are a TON of work, for 50+ years, and you need to take that into account before you get one! They are a commitment for life, please remember that ❤️

video_title: Why People Loves Macaw Parrot So Much
video_description: shorts #macaw #birds #pet #cute #cuteanimals #pets #animal #animals #animalfacts #animalshorts #animaldocumentary #nature ...
text_content: Macaws are so cool! Though they are very expensive and they need so much attention and they are some of the most rehomed parrots. They are really cool birds but you should do your research and look at where you are and where you will be in the many years to come before getting one to prevent another bird being rehomed. With correct care and training a Macaw will be a loyal and adorable friend for many years to come!

video_title: Why People Loves Macaw Parrot So Much
video_description: shorts #macaw #birds #pet #cute #cuteanimals #pets #animal #animals #animalfacts #animalshorts #animaldocumentary #nature ...
text_content: The fact parrots can so easily be bought is really sad. I don’t think people understand what they’re getting into when they get parrots of this size. They are a massive commitment and require a large chunk of your life to be dedicated to them if you only buy one and not a pair. They are in my opinion the absolute best pet you can own when they are happy and bonded to you but they can be extremely exhausting to care for

text_content:
I get so happy seeing your videos. It makes me sad how often these animals are misunderstood or mistreated. I used to help care for a dozen parrots in a private collection, 2 were Hyacinth macaws and another was a harlequin. They all had their wings clipped and it broke my heart so much I didn't stay there long.
The ironic part was that she was very wealthy and had all the material things they could need, but she skimped on the quality of their mental and emotional care. They were like toys she kept in a toy box that she'd play with for a while and then put away.
It still makes sad to think about. I hope they're okay.

video_title: I still love him so much though 🥰 #pyro #macaw #bird
text_content: I parrot sat a large Cockatoo for several weeks while the owners went on vacation. That was enough for me. When the owners returned and took him back, I was so done with birds. That Cockatoo was loud, messy, needy, feisty and ornery. Because he didn’t know me, he never showed any modicum of friendliness. He also didn’t talk. A very pretty bird, though.

video_title: I still love him so much though 🥰 #pyro #macaw #bird
text_content: It's truly amazing how many resources these birds need and deserve to live their best domesticated life.  Thank you for educating us on birds, bird needs, bird wants, and best bird parent practices.  I wish I'd had the resources social media has provided when I kept birds.  There's a lot of misinformation out there, so I appreciate that you take the time to clarify facts and fiction and to show us the reality of your home life with these precious companions.  Birds aren't easy to keep up with, but if you've got the passion, dedication, space, and resources to entertain one, they can make for fabulous friends if you put in the time and effort.  Y'all please don't try to keep birds without assured connections to other trustworthy, more experienced owners for advice, especially after-hours, and securing a vet before you need one as most vets don't treat avian friends. ❤

video_title: Parrot On A Mission To His Room | Mikey The Macaw
video_description: Mikey The Macaw climbs many stairs to get back to his room. Just through this was just too sweet not to share. Follow Mikey: ...
text_content:
Hello from August 2020. 
For some crazy reason, YouTube has recently recommended this video to all of you so before you leave your comments, let us answer a few questions. 

Yes, he can fly, very very well, he actually free flies which means he is outside in parks for around an hour every single day soaring through the skies freely with his lover Mia. 

He chooses to walk and not fly up the stairs due to he is smart enough to know how large his wing span is, he knows if he attempted to fly, he won’t have enough space and may hurt himself. 

No, this is not a closet... It was actually a double bedroom, we just choose to keep a clothing rail in it. This aviary has been taken down and a larger powdercoated steel one has been built. 

No, he is not in a dark room with no natural light, there is a large double window in here which in this video is covered up due to it was night time. 

Feel free to watch any of the other videos uploaded over the last 2 years which should show all the points highlighted above. 

Thanks for watching.

text_content:
I live in Brazil's capital Brasília and every single morning I see a couple of Canindé Macaws flying over my house and making their characteristic noises. I guess they're at least 30 or 40 years old and they're completely wild, because Brasília is full of trees in the residential areas so they adapted very well and not only them but there's also a third macaw, a small group of green parrots, 2 or 3 toucans and a whole family of marmosets, all which come here to my backyard to eat fruits from the trees hahaha

God bless you and your babies! Much love from Brazil 🇧🇷🙌

video_title: How to get Mikey and Mia inside…
text_content:
I love when pets are actually properly trained and house free-range like this ❤

A LOT of "cage" ones can actually be trained to chill in the whole house, as most pets are pack / group species.

Once they establish home is their nest and you pet-proof what you need, you just chill with them. They'll potty where you train them too, and are happy to be and explore with you.

A big Thanks for all your information

text_content: Thank you so much for this , I will be getting back to you. I  Have a Military Macaw  his name is Paco, short for Apocalypse . You have already answered so many questions I have,  and I would like to ask you more . I don't approve of caging birds but my wife had him when we got together, he was a rescue but sadly my wife passed away  only 3 yrs after we met . For the past 5 yrs he's lived in the corner of my living room , that's not to say he has a bad life , I interact with him many times a day. He has perches inside and out . He talks , only to me , I play guitar to him most nights , he likes New york -New york , Da Da Dada Da . The problem is I need to re-home him as he will live decades more than me  (I'm 66 ). I have tried Craigs list but , imagine you had a child and knew that you would not be around to raise him , how hard would that be to 'hand him off' and would anyone really  be good enough ? Any advice would be appreciated,  again thank you for that video is was much more informative than anything I have watched.

video_description: "Why are the animals in an enclosure?" "Why can't they simply be released?" are common ...
text_content:
Editing for YouTube, especially if you do it yourself and speaking in a non-native language is really hard!  Great work putting this piece together.  Great Green Macaws are one of our most cherished species of Macaw at Flight Club Foundation.  In fact, the illegal trade out of Columbia brought a Great Green from the Darien Gap to the United States in a smuggling ring.  My friend began a reintroduction program in the Darien Gap and now 12 pairs are successfully raising chicks.  It's been a 30 year program almost nearing its end.  

My question is in regard to numbers.  I know Costa Rican Great Green and Ecuadorean Great Greens are really the last two strongholds, is my understanding.  Down to hundreds left?  My understanding is there are a few thousand left, but the increased pressure from colonization is really the biggest issue (deforestation).  I also know the local pet trade is a serious issue since the birds are available and the people are living right there.  

The devastation I saw in that sattelite image was profound and needs to be shared far, far, far more.  I work with Dr. George Olah, PhD and his work discovering the root causes of worldwide parrot decline. In addition, I know Janice Boyd is an avid Great Green advocate.   I did not see the name of your sanctuary, perhaps I missed it?  

I

Ever since I was a child, I dreamed of parrots and prairie dogs. I've been living with prairie dogs since 1997. But you have to create a special habitat and design one that can be transported on vacation. 
Living with parrots must be much more difficult: you can't hide them in your T-shirt to go to the movies; it's impossible to get into a hotel room with 5 animals hidden on top of my wife and me; 1 parrot makes a lot more noise than a whole family of prairie dogs.
Still, I love these magnificent birds. The black palm cockatoo is truly magnificent. The Hyacinth macaw was the bird of my dreams, and I still find it so splendid.

Sorry I don't fit your need, I'd love to have adopted him, he would have been good for my dog.  Of course, I'm too far away for any of your animals, which is why I don't share: my friends and family are too far from LA, too.  I am certain he needs a friend other than me to be happy. text_content: Learning your bird's boundaries is a huge part of things. I think many birds end up in rescue situations because of this. People don't understand that it takes time to learn how to read them and when a bird bites they blame it on the bird being "aggressive" rather than their own lack of patience and understanding. After years with my Amazon, I have learned that he can communicate to me what he is comfortable with. It takes time and patience to learn their body language and build trust.

video_title: Parrots & Biting
video_description: Whenever my birds & I meet new people, one of their first questions or fears will be about bites. The truth is, they don't want to ...
text_content: I work behavioral issue macaws to help them become adoption candidates. This is very true. Most bites I’ve gotten have come from fear. I’ve worked with macaws who have been through insane amounts of abuse, I’ve worked with birds that would bite everyone and everything, yet they never hurt me to the point of needing stitches or permanent disfigurement. I’ve gotten some nasty bites here and there, I’ve collected a few scars even, but it’s a far cry from the real damage these guys can do. They are pretty gentle by nature. I love seeing how they transform and learn better boundaries as well. I learn  how to keep that bird from feeling the need to bite. Birds will bite but most of the time they give very clear warnings before hand. It definitely is about knowing your bird as well, birds can bite not just because they’re scared but also because they are over stimulated. Be gentle, patient, and kind with a biting bird. Most will learn boundaries over time or at least you will learn how to pick up on the warning signs.

text_content:
I will say this as an umbrella cockatoo dad of five years:

My bird (Einstein) is one of the most affectionate and emotionally intelligent, if not needy, animals I’ve ever owned. He explores with his beak and having a safe and fruitful relationship with him has been all about learning how to read his body language, understand his precursors to naughty or stress behavior, and setting/enforcing boundaries when needed. 

However, with that said…. He has bitten me and my sister very badly quite a few times. Being a bird owner is about knowing that yes, you can expect nips, but you need to be prepared for the occasional major bite and approach it with the understanding that going forward…. Learn from WHY you got bit. 

For every single major bite I’ve sustained from Einstein, I can 100% say that it was my fault for not listening to his body language. You have to know your bird inside and out, but to a degree I disagree that you shouldn’t expect proper bites. I find that to be unrealistic 

Love your birds by the way, it’s obvious that you take incredible care of them and know their body languages so so so well. You’re a great bird dad

text_content: As an ornithologist, I would have to say, you may be making your bird hormonal by petting below the neck.  Birds also train you more than you think.  If they scream and you run to them to keep them quiet, you are training them to scream louder and longer until they get your attention.  I made this mistake with a very loud and obnoxious Moluccan Cockatoo.  I grew to resent this bird until I learned that my behaviour trained him to scream.  Moluccan’s can be as loud as Hyacinths. I was miserable until I learned not to run to see him whenever he screamed.  You’re right though.....large birds are like toddlers on crack that never grow up.  I like the chaos!  The screaming and destruction......not so much.  But all pets can be loud.  And even a dog will chew your best shoes if you leave them out.  The trick is you have to control your environment.  Make one room in your house an aviary, with tiled floors and walls, access to a hose, and have a drain in the floor.  Buy baby toys. They are fairly durable, or find stainless steel toys.  It is possible to own big birds and live harmoniously.  You just have to sacrifice and plan ahead.

thank you for this video

a lady was trying to give away her 2 year old Hyacinth Macaw to me ....

I am glad I saw your video before taking on such a huge commitment and responsibility, that I don't have a space for nor the time for ...

taking in small parrots is fine-ish
but I should never think of getting a parrot unless I have a private house with a garden I guess.
thank you for everything. Even with all the intense care and behaviour I still want a Macaw, but can't do anything about it now
I'd feel awful keeping such a large bird in a cage  when I can't be around due to work.

text_content: This video is not an exaggeration. I have an African Grey and this all goes for them as well. My grey is with me on my shoulder 12 hours a day. When I have to go out for a large amount of time I have a sitter for her. I tried out so many sitters before I found one that actually CARED about my bird. It isn't that easy. If my bird is awake she does not want to be alone, or in her cage.She is very sensitive and wants love, love, love, and more love. When she misbehaves (and she does) you cannot hold a grudge or else you will have a very depressed bird. Then there is hormone season. All of that sweetness, is gone, and you wonder if it will E V E R return. You need to have thick skin and you need to be very empathetic and forgiving and YES they will bite you.

text_content:
The problem is the off-the-shelf cage rod panels are not welded. The pieces are fit together, but loose. 

I would recommend looking into smaller avian cage manufacturers. Not only will you find a better price, but you can usually customize your cages to fit your birds and your space better. Alternatively, local welders, or even a rural school with a welding program, would also be good options to look into. 

As someone like yourself who keeps a lot of a specific type of animal, it's something I wish I had known about when I started. Would have saved me a lot of money in the long run!

video_description: If your Hyacinth Macaw wants to play! #play #playful #happy #cute #cuteanimals.
text_content:
Beautiful baby...her/his flight feathers don't appear to be fully open yet!
I raised B&G, Green Wing, and Military macaws for some years before taking them all down to Panama, releasing them on Isla Coiba before it was established as a National Park in 1991.  I worked with the Panamanian government throughout the '70s--'80s doing research toward establishing the island as a national park...Isla Coiba has the largest density of macaws anywhere in Latin America.  There were times I saw flocks of over 25 macaws flying about this pristine (uninhabited) island. My research there, and other locals was basis for my PhD...until politics intervened my dream was re-establishing macaws in their original range throughout the West Indies.
I've some great tales of rescuing baby Hyacinth macaws from giant trees (illegally) cut down in the Northern Pantanal region...turning them over to indigenous elders who then entrusted them to local children already well accustomed to rearing parrots/macaws...almost got shot several times by parrot poacher, too!  
You hyacinth is clever, beautiful and will give you and your family life-long joy...take care of and protect "her"!

text_content: There was a Hyacinth for a long time at my local bird store where a lot of birds my parents have had over the years (including my current bird of 23 years).  It was always a highlight of the trip to play with him.  Like Hymie in this video, this macaw really liked going on people's shoulders.  So I would rarely pick him up and just play with him instead.  Eventually though I figured I could retain control by gripping both of his feet.  Eventually he was sold, and I hope he's happy.  He was extremely friendly and playful, but needed a firm hand to set boundaries.  Always thought Hyacinths were a lot of fun, but given how huge they are, how much they cost, and how long they live, I would never get one myself.

text_content: Great vid. We have a Green Wing and yep he was a shoulder rusher too....trained him and now he doesn't even feel comfortable up there after 3yrs.....he likes to stay on forearms....still working to get him to be on your hand with thumb over one foot....some success there, always a work in progress. Anyway, lots of people think they want the big birds......maybe you could do videos on how hard they really are. Like how much they eat, how quickly they destroy $30+ toys, how needy they get when bored, how noisy they can be when bored or even just crazy noisy they are when happy. Don't forget the messes they make...and don't forget big bird equals big poop and lots of it. Also how about how smart, mischievous, and manipulating they can really be. When our GW is bored and wants to play with the other birds like our cockatiel, he will go let him out of his cage. How about how quirky they can be as individuals, if we don't change out one of our GW stands poop papers down below it, he will pull the papers off himself and throw them around. Im sure big bird owners have lots of fun(?)quirky bird stories. All that said he is a member of the family and we couldn't be without him......but some days he pushes that pretty hard....🤦

text_content: I have a red crowned and yellow crowned, both arrived as juveniles. text_content: That's true, for the most part, that Amazons tend to me a one-person bird, but this trait varies with the species.  They are moody, though, during breeding season and the hot three that are most tempermental are the Yellow-naped, Double Yellow-Headed, and Blue Fronted.   I agree that they do choose their owner, or person to bond with, which makes it difficult to find a sitter or someone to take care of them when their favorite person is not around.  If you're looking for an all-around friendly parrot for the family, a cockatoo is a better choice but they are super noisy and give off a lot of dust.

text_content: Ive had my blue fronted amazon for coming up 10 years and i love him to bits. I keep him in a double macaw cage with the divider taken out. The cage is 6foot tall 7foot wide and 3 and a half feet deep. I feed my bird pellets with a splash of nuts and seeds thrown in then i make a fresh mixture of harico beans spinach cabvage boiled eggs carrot and i also mix in some of the pellets i crush to a powder then i bind it all together with sweet potato and i also give him a bowl of fruit whatever is in season (he loves star fruit and lychee 's) so i have 3 different pits of food in his cage. He's healthy and has great plumage.

text_content: My Amazon joined my family as a rescue when I was 5. He turned aggressive to my parents but bonded to me, ever since I was young. I wasn’t allowed to handle him until I got older and then I learned he would never bite me. Now, I’m an adult and take him out daily. He knows colors and can pick a colored object on command, or the next color in a pattern. I think he understands a lot of English vocabulary, though he’s not very talkative. We thing he’s 30-40 or more years old now. I hope he lives for a lot longer.

text_content:
I have a very large beautiful male yellow nape  named Cricket.  I have had him for 27 years (he is 28) and I got him from a sanctuary where he'd been placed after being abused.  I think HR is the perfect parrot, but it took a lot of time and work to gain his trust, and he still barely tolerates men.  

He is relatively quiet, and sing and talks and doesn't scream except rarely and only if he senses danger like a hawk or helicopter or cat.and is incredibly loving in his way and extremely affectionate.  I am disabled an have several chronic illnesses including brittle diabetes, and he learned very early to alert me to low blood sugar emergencies by rubbing vigorously on my cheek and saying "it's OK!"  "You alright?" and not stopping until I ate or drank (of course he got jackpot treats!). If it was late or bedtime he would often not go  in his cage, but run to the bedroom and station himself on the headboard of the bed and would want to watch me all night. Getting him in his cage was hard on those nights!   I live in NYC and he is a fixture in the neighborhood,  If he isn't seen for a  few days during good weather,  people come and ask about him.  He constantly flirts with the neighborhood women calling them to. "come see the birdie", and telling them that they're beautiful. His only flaw is that he's messy,but I wouldn't change a thing about him.  He is a true blessing in my life,  and he also brings smiles to almost everyone who sees him!

text_content:
So i work at this bird store near me and i have worked there for almost 4 years. Almost every bird i have met has always been good with me even surprising the owners which is kind of amazing. My boss calls me the "Bird Whisperer" and almost every customer knows this and when we get new birds that are being surrendered i'm the first to try and befriend the bird. About 2 months ago a lady had called our store saying she sees a parrot in a pine tree, so of course my coworker tells me to go down and see if i can get this bird down. Of course i agreed and went down without knowing the type of bird it was. I get down to the area and there was a Yellow Naped Amazon high in this tree so im trying to figure out how to get this bird down without scaring him off. well i stood near the tree for about 30 minutes just talking to this bird when he flys down to the ground and walks over to me! I go up to the big guy and he steps up immediately and i just put him into my car with no issues. Of course me and my coworkers post this bird online to see if anyone is going to claim this beautiful bird, a couple weeks pass and no one came forward. During the time we were looking for the owner, i decided to give him a name for the moment which became Jasper. in only a couple days Jasper learned his name and became a mush with me, cuddles all the time, let me put a harness on him, eats pellets and chop with no issues. The only problem that Jasper has is he bonded to me, he tolerates 3 of my coworkers so Now its been almost 3 months and we just decided instead of finding him a new home to keep him as a mascot. Me and my boss also made a deal since im the one who found him, since i live in an apartment, when i get my own place i can take Jasper home! 

So i guess Jasper the Yellow Naped Amazon chose me as his mom! I never really had an actual opinion on amazons but ever since i met Jasper he has been such a great bird and will be a great addition to my flock!

text_content: I am my Amazons third and last owner.  I got him when he was 7, he is 36 now.  He loves me and hates all others.  He is not a good talker and can be pretty grumpy, and lucky for me, he is on the quiet side.  I was able to live in a lot of apartments with him with no complaints from neighbors….but that isn’t the norm.  My wish is that all people thinking of getting a parrot do their research and make sure they know what they are getting into:  all the healthy food prep, the mess, the noise, the destruction, exotic vet bills, and all the attention they need to keep them happy and well adjusted.  It’s a major commitment, and obviously a lot of people buying parrots do not consider it, or we wouldn’t see such a heartbreaking number of parrots in need of new homes.  Please PLEASE remember these are intelligent, loving creatures who bond very strongly with their owners, it’s not fair to them to not be given the care they deserve, or to be bounced from person to person.

text_content:
If you think you want a parrot be aware that they live a very long time and also need many hours of attention each day. Do your research and give a new home to a bird from a rescue centre. Do not get a bird if you are out at work all day, they do not deserve to be shut in a cage on their own. Give them love and understanding and they will return it, but by their very nature they will be getting up to mischief and needing constant attention.
A cage should not be a prison but a sanctuary that your bird can use to sleep and feed in.

text_content: Amazons are excellent talkers! I’m owned by a goffins(7yrs) and medium sulphur crested cockatoo(23yrs). My goffin, Dingleberry, jabbers a lot like George. My sulphur crested cockatoo,Sprout, was very sick as a baby and has cerebral palsy. She didn’t ever talk and in all honesty, we thought she was retarded. I don’t mean that in a bad or derogatory manner. A few years ago, I had a home health nurse she loved! The nurse came over one day and didn’t see me. When she asked the home health aid where I was and before she could answer, Sprout looked right at her and said “She’s sick. Check the bathroom!” It was as plain as day after 20 years of thinking she was truly retarded! She doesn’t chatter at all. But, she has a large vocabulary and will tell you things. The thing my neighbors get the biggest kick out of is she will give you a list of what she wants if you tell her you are going to the store. She 💗taking a shower! That’s a good thing since cockatoos are so dusty! The thing that surprises people the most is cockatoos love to cuddle and each one has a very distinct personality. Mine, like George, are also camera shy☺️.

text_content: He reminds me of my Oscar who's 23 now. I've had him 3 and half years. Oscar had a rough life but he's coming into it. Oscar is a bit bigger than your guy. You are lucky as Oscar was abused during his first half of his life. He can be a bully as well mine enjoys his TV programs like an old man. We both have anxiety, we both have PTSD we are both agoraphobic so needless to say we were made to be together lol. We have the same bond. He's my only friend after life went belly up for me. I've always taken in parrots who have had tough backgrounds. I've always helped them get back to a good baseline and find them good homes. But Oscar is definitely here for the long haul. I also have another parrot roommate who's a sun conure who's fully flighted very agile he's 7 now. Oscar has all his flight feathers but he's a bit too heavy to fly and just really hasn't built up his wing muscles due to being cage bound for a good part of his life. He will get aggressive with other men as he was definitely abused by a man or men. We have grown to have a good bond and are very used to each other's routines. The sun conure his name is Capri and Oscar do get along most of the time and Oscar will show dominance and scare him off to let him know that's enough. Which works until next time lol. They are all I have and I'm all they have, and when life gets too much to deal with I have them.

text_content: I have a Blue Front Amazon.  He hardly talks at all, he is actually very quiet, and wants to murder everyone but me.  I think it is very important that people consider their expectations when getting a bird, because there are NO guarantees - especially when it comes to talking.  I know people with African Grey parrots who are not good talkers.  You never know what kind of bird you are going to wind up with.  Before I got mine, I considered all of my reasons for wanting a parrot, and decided that I would accept him for who he was and would be committed to him for as long as he lives.  Sure, it would be cool if he talked more, but I don’t love him any less because he doesn’t.

video_title: A discussion on "the hot three" - blue fronted, yellow nape, and double yellow headed Amazon parrots
text_content:
I have a Phillip. Her name is Princess. Around 40 years old.  She needs a nail trim.  Her beak has been clean for decades. She was egg-bound at one point in her life.  But with vet help, she is very good. 

Fiesty is an understatement. Ha! She loves my daughter, fortunately.  She was my mother's, and she adored me then. When my mother passed, I got the bird. And she suddenly hated me.  Literally chasing me down the hall like a little dog.  But that didn't bother me.  I understand that she lost a loved one too. I'm just glad she loves my daughter.

video_title: A discussion on "the hot three" - blue fronted, yellow nape, and double yellow headed Amazon parrots
text_content: I have one of the hot three—Nico the blue fronted Amazon and he lives up to the reputation! Nico was in need of a home and idiot me said I would foster him. It was quite a learning curve (bites, attacking, possessive of my daughter his “person”). But here we are 11 years later…part of the flock and happy 😅.

video_title: A discussion on "the hot three" - blue fronted, yellow nape, and double yellow headed Amazon parrots
text_content:
Good luck tomorrow Philip! 

Everybirdy is different, our double yellow head isn’t possessive or even that unpredictable, but he has clear boundaries. I am allowed to pet him, others are encouraged to admire him from a respectful distance, which he enjoys. He loves to watch a party. 😂

text_content: Are his nostrils clogged? I don’t know much about a parrots nasal anatomy, but it looked like there was dark crummy stuff in there, if I didn’t see it right, please educate me 😅

video_title: A discussion on "the hot three" - blue fronted, yellow nape, and double yellow headed Amazon parrots
text_content:
The "hot 3" sounds like my spicy Quaker. She's been a challenge. One day, when her time here is done, (hopefully not for 20+ years) I'll open my heart and home to another spicy baby. Hopefully a rescue. I feel like she has taught me a lifetime of patience, commitment and loyalty and never giving up on someone you love. I think I'll be prepared for another case of a spicy bird that needs extra understanding. Given how many Amazons are in rescues, and their notorious spicy nature, it sounds like one might be in my future, many years from now. Knowing their like mine solidifies my plan.

I took need to blunt my birds beak because of her frequency of unpredictable bitting.

video_title: The Painted Bunting: The Most Beautiful Bird in North America?
video_description: In this video, I feature the Painted Bunting, perhaps the most beautiful bird in the United States. It certainly is a striking bird, to say ...
text_content:
In 2016, the first painted bunting that stayed in the yard was a female in the 2016-17 season. The next year, one female, and a male with a distinct pure yellow back, and light orange- red below. The back and wings have a strong copper tint. By 2019-20, a more ‘ typical’ deeper red below, and and strongly green back joined the two. Each year, more stuck around. Last year 2024-25, there were nine- at least, since that was the maximum number visible at once looking outside, from the desk. The pale colored bird (maybe the same one ?) has been coming for seven years. Last season, of the nine there were 5 greenies, and 4 males. Two of the males were ‘typical’. The pale, copper-washed one was the third, the fourth was ‘typical’, except for scattered bright yellow patches on the red breast. The females were also variable, mostly do to the lighting. One was normal green above and dull green (not light yellow-green) below. It did have a swath of yellow green on the sides. Sometimes, but not always, there’d be a bluish tint on the head, probably from the lighting. Being at the desk with the binoculars, it was easy see the differences. The last week of April they begin to thin out, by May 4th there were only 2 females, May 6 was the last day I saw one. Last year was the first time carefully studying them. i set the feeder, water fountain and dishes, and trimmed the foliage in a few spots, all to help in watching them from inside, without disturbing them. 
All nine survived till they took of in May, which;for such a small bird, I find surprising. 
The first one of this season, a ‘typical’ male showed up in late September, later that afternoon, a female arrived. As of October 8th, there are definitely two males and the one female. Interesting, last year, on the same day, there were three, all males. The pictures are lousy, but they were all regular males, none pale, or with yellow patches below. I have seen a male as early as mid August, snd the latest female on May 11 th. I don’t think any of those are the ‘winter’ residents.
When I show friends the birds, and tell them that they come for the winter, I have received odd looks or questioned. I mean, the wintering flock arrives in September, and leave in May, being here nearly eight months! Plus Miami’s summer rainy  season is from May to the end of October, or most of November in a few recent years. Better than American Redstart (a warbler) they start showing up the last few days of July, and one or two (or three, in 2020-21) visit the yard all winter, with the latest in the yard on June 5th. Long winter-or short summer up north! 
Anyway, they are wonderful birds. Years ago, never could have imagined I’d get to have them in the yard all season. I’m a birder, have been since 1988. And I’ve lived in Miami since 1992. At least for me, buntings have become easier to find in my area. No, I’m not talking about yard birds. I mean birding in the field. In the 90s they were around, but in smaller numbers. In the mid 2000s, there seemed to have been an uptick. By about 2012, definitely more common. 
Data shows them, as a whole, declining. There was a long-term local banding study in Miami-Dade County from the mid 1960s-mid 1970s. The amount of banded birds the first few years was huge. Up to 20 wintering at a feeding station. By the last few years, less than ten! It may be that they’ve rebounded. Use of native plants by gardeners and city planners in the early 90s was negligible, today it s much more common, and public planting, at least in the city of Miami, now must include more than 50% of trees being native to Miami-Dade county. Maybe that could be helping…I’ve been careful to plant natives, including ones they like.
Then again, although they live in natural areas, I’ve seen them several times right in the city. In 2006- 7, a male (pale, similar to the one I see, would visit the courtyard in south Beach where I lived- and that spot wax definitely urban. Did not have a feeder, and couldn’t garden. But that one definitely wintered, as I’d see it occasionally from the window for several months. 
Anyway, hope the info is interesting to anybody that actually read that novel I wrote.
Thanks again for making the video, I really enjoyed it!
