video_description: Discover everything Painted Buntings eat – from seeds and berries to surprising protein-packed snacks! This complete diet ...
text_content:
Great to hear that you have more buntings this year. For me, this is the best year for painted buntings in the yard. The first wintering one was a female in 2015-16. From the next season though 2019-20 there was one typical male and one female. In 2020-21, a second male, with a very yellow back, with a faint red flecking arrived. The same one (?) has come back each year since. There’s been 2-3 green ones also. Last year, there were 3 female types. One had a bright blue shine around the head. This one was almost always very close to the female cardinal for some reason. The only male I saw was the yellow-backed one, and not regularly like the other three.  I’ll let you know about this year, after some problems, and solutions, I’ve been working out in the last few years.
From 2020-23, I was battling Eurasian Collared-Doves. Never witnessed aggression with them-Instead huge numbers (100 or more at a time!) would show up. Unlike any of the other yard birds, they’d panic for no obvious reason, all the time. This scared everyone else,of course. Not joking, half a bag of seed would be gone in a few hours. In time, I just ended up placing little bits of seed near the window on the ground, and/or stopped entirely for a few days when the doves would show up. This summer, I re-discovered a squirrel-proof bird feeder I bought in 2008. It didn’t attract much of anything back then (squirrels even ignored it). Found it, cleaned it up, and put it up where I could see from my desk. The few collared-doves are still around, as well as mournings, and one white-winged,all ignore the feeder. All the others, including the buntings, use it. Quickly another problem arose. House sparrows also stay away-until seed is put out. Within a week, very large numbers hung around, pretty much all day. Cardinals and blue jays simply pushed them aside. Not so with the buntings. They would drive them off. I would even see a female sparrow just sit on the feeder for long periods, pecking at any bunting that tried to land. Something needed to be done, immediately! I emptied the feeder first. It’s probably not good to have hordes of any species constantly around. So many would attract predators, bird droppings cover everything, and diseases could spread.  Went on YouTube to find a solution. A channel called ‘Badgerland Birds’ had a video that deals with this issue that looked promising. Hanging filaments of silver twine around the feeder deters them. I bent a metal clothes hanger into a circular shape, installing it just above the feeder. Attached several strands to the hanger, tying a heavy screw to the ends of each. This keeps birds from getting tangled in the strands, and also prevents individual pieces from getting tangled up. Not any paid endorsement… but it completely solved the problem! Before I came inside, one of the females was already eating. The sparrows tried to get to the feeder. They’d hover, gather on nearby branches, never actually landing. This was on October 24th. One female alone a few days later managed to get in. After a minute it flew away. Not once since have any others been successful, and now none even try. A few do come, eating from the ground below. Within two days, the cardinals came back to the feeder, and the next day the blue jays. They all come and go normally now. I’m not sure exactly why, but it worked better than I imagined!
As of today (Nov 19th) there are at least eight buntings. For a while, even with the sparrows, one green-backed and one yellow-backed male. There’s been five greens. Sometimes all are around the feeder at the same time. Early on Saturday (16th), there were two green-backed males eating next to each other, with the yellow-backed one a branch a few inches away. Definitely eight. Starting yesterday, a green one with a strong yellow triangle-shaped patch on the back that stands out visits. I don’t think I overlooked it. If not, then there are nine! I work (try to) at my desk a lot, so there’s been ample time to watch the buntings. The two males often come separately, each with two or more greens. They also come from different directions. 
Who knows, there may be a few groups of these birds in my pretty unremarkable Little Havana/ Shenandoah neighborhood. With your numbers, maybe Painted Buntings are doing better. I definitely see larger numbers out birding than back in the 1990s. Finally, some good news?

text_content:
All "New Color" Canaries owe their expanded color range to hybrids between domestic Canaries and South America's Red Hooded Siskin (Spinus cucullatus). F1 hybrid females are usually subfertile (a term favored by biologists over "infertile"), but a % of F1 hybrid males are fertile. The early color bred Canaries were known as "Red Factor Canaries", but many other varieties are now bred, including Agates, Opals, Bronzes, and Dimorphics. 

Red Hooded Siskins, like their European Siskin cousins, can with patience often be hand tamed. Both are more social than Canaries, and sometimes bond with people even as parent reared wild trapped adults, as Konrad Lorenz noted for his native Siskins some 65 years ago. Males are considerably less likely to be destructive or problematic during the breeding season than many male Canaries. Their song is very pleasing and they add "wild bird" phrases to the songs of their hybrids. Unfortunately, overtrapping of wild specimens for hybridizing has drastically reduced many wild populations. Enforcement of protective legislation here in the US has been poor and often erratic.

video_description: The Half Moon Conure, also known as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
Every conure has their own personalities. 
My female peachy or half moon 🌙 conure only gets noisy if she see's a predator outside. Then she FREAKS OUT ! One other time she gets noisy in a different way, is when she pleasures herself ( which is often) against a inanimate object she will make a weird noise that if you don't see what's she's doing, you'd think she is in Trouble. 
My Peachy does NOT say 1 word. 
I will say Peanut is very inquisitive about things vs my other 13 parrots. She is sooooo smart in that department. 
Her roommate and platonic friend Kiwi, my male Sun Conure is noisy and loud.
The worse thing is his repetitive sound he makes over and over and over. 
There is very little variation in his vocalization.  It drives me absolutely 
crazy ! Like parrot experts tell you,
Do NOT yell at your parrot. It achieves 
NOTHING but a even noisier bird.
Overall, conures are GREAT 1st parrots but I recommend a male over female. 
Males are better tempered. Thats not to say someone can't have a very sweet female. Females WILL eventually lay eggs and once that starts, their little personalities will change from sweet to
aggressive at certain times. I'm not a expert on parrots, but I have enough of them for roughly 15 years to gain some experience/knowledge about them.

video_title: Peach Fronted Conure, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: The Peach Fronted Conure, often confused as the Orange Fronted Conure, is a slightly lesser known Conure in the pet trade.
text_content:
I’ve had mine for 22 years.  He is a doll but not cuddly, I cannot skritch his head.  He is super smart, a pretty good talker.  Very polite and not that messy for a parrot.  His only loud moments are his alarm call when someone is in the yard or I walk past him with a broom, yardstick, etc.  All conures are “nippy” AKA bitey….lol.  My Peach Fronted only bites under extreme duress.  He has really never bitten me.  When I got him, I asked him “can I call you Swee’Pea” and he said “ok”❤❤   He loves to hang out on my shoulder, he laughs and flirts.  
He loves music and has his own playlist he dances to.  
I think they are awesome little birds.

text_content:
Oh, Momo is very a much a nipper, but conures overall can get kinda bitey. That being said, when you know how to read em, you can usually avoid most bites. She's cuddly, but very much on her own terms. Sometimes she's into head scritches, and sometimes she'd just rather sit up next to your arm or something and preen. We keep her in our living room, that way she has access to both a window, TV, and that's usually where atleast one of us is hanging out within eyesight. She has a habit of climbing down from her cage, less now as she's gotten older. Usually happens when someone is not in the room and she gets bored from her toys and stuff. Typically she'll make her way to the main table and climb to where I sit usually. 

She's pretty quiet, but has flocked called for people once in a while. Usually she's her loudest (and kinda scariest) is when she spots like a cat, hawk, or person (Pest control, etc) outside. That, or when you talk on the phone and she tries to figure out what your doing. Never really got her to talk, but again she isn't super loud all the time. I do sometimes get her to make kissing noises! Plus, you can teach em other tricks (I've gotten her to spin!).

The Peach is the king of conures, very smart, very reading...its not the most playful bird, it's more reserved than most conures, but they do like attention.

Under scored heavily i reckon

video_title: Do Macaws Make Good Pets?
text_content: Lots of good info I wasn’t aware of! I’m not a fan of keeping non-domesticated pet birds in the first place— but I know people do love them. The only “good” thing about these animals being constantly rehomed is if you want one (and you can meet their needs) you can ethically adopt one.

video_title: Do Macaws Make Good Pets?
text_content: I love to look at them, but there’s no way I would ever take one on, I have two cockatiels and they make enough noise and mess!!  They’ve turned my house into a house sized aviary as they are allowed free when I’m home , I’ve no wood, paint or wallpaper left intact,  but they’re my birds  and I’m apparently part of their flock 😂 they will stay with me until I’m gone ❤

video_title: Do Macaws Make Good Pets?
text_content: I wasn't aware of any of this..to keep a bird like that in a small cage is sad.

video_title: Do Macaws Make Good Pets?
text_content:
Macaws may not make great pets, but they make fabulous children. Mine has been with me for about 10 years now, we passed puberty.
My last one I had for over 20 years until she passed away from an egg binding. 

Is it for everyone? No. For me? Yes. 
(And I have a rule with all pets to only take rescues.)

text_content: My  3 year old eclectus came from a good home. Yet they were a bit old school and stated they just grabbed her or pryed her feet off of whatever. Long story short I needed more training! I am gratefull for finding you guys. She now comes on car rides with me! Permission based not domination based. Target training progressed into fun wich progressed into her trusting me and actually wanted to step up which progressed into her now only wanting me to get to my wife 😢 whatever we have only come this far because of the important advice you guys give!

video_title: How To Get a Macaw to STOP BITING and START STEPPING UP!
video_description: Today Sabrina has brought in her blue and gold macaw to work with us on permission based training and simple step ups.
text_content: Thanks to Dave & Jamieleigh, I did a version of this with two rescue CAGs. We did LOTS of targeting for what seemed like a  l o n g  time. I didn't even ask them to step up. We were building trust and I was learning and respecting their No. The first one spontaneously stepped onto my hand, when I reached into his cage for his food bowl, calm as a cucumber. The second one was more like Dave's demo. Point is, they KNEW I knew and respected their No and so could trust me. BT for the win! Again.

text_content:
Oddly enough I discovered this blowing in the face when getting bitten and they won’t let go…. I have an adolescent middle school/pubescent/teenager 4-5 month old male kitten who’s kinda in the same hormone stages the mcaw is. 
He gets crazy, moody, bitey, spicy, bursting with energy, and sometimes he’ll get so worked up or he doesn’t have a good attitude about having his nails clipped & he’ll clamp down on you with his kitten needle teeth and won’t let go! 😅 it seriously hurts! The most effective thing I’ve tried is blowing hard into his ears and it turns off  the bite switch! He looks kinda puzzled as to why I did that and “ what’s the problem? “ 😂
I love him dearly and he’s a good boy when he’s run and played out his kitten energy! ❤

text_content: I hope Louie loves his new life. How sad to live in a cage for 26 years. It is rare for any type of parrot/macaw to not need a cage but not locked in one 24/7 for years. I visited sanctuaries that are supposed to be wonderful but people would be appalled if they saw the conditions at some “bird sanctuaries”. It is disturbing to know that some birds are relinquished to places that are not much better than their neglectful former homes. I think the best life is in a good home with people that fully understand what a bird needs to be healthy and happy. Good luck with Louis and his new life with you. Thank goodness you found each other. 😊

text_content: I now have 3 macaws. My girl picked up what we thought was a plucking issue after the death of our bonded cockatiels so I took in a pair of Macaws and she is sooooo happy and it’s super fun sharing our lives with our feathered family. These birds are so demanding but we knew this wayyyyyy before committing to ownership and we knew the work would harden when we took two more in but we do both work from home and we’re fast workers so we have plenty of time to give them everything they need plus more (the three of them joined us on Date Night tonight). They help us make new toys for their indoor aviary.

text_content:
Love the longer content! I saw your post about how life has been taking its toll and especially about losing a parent, and I just want to say a huge THANK YOU. Even with all the hardships, you still managed to deliver, and that means so much.

You mentioned feeling less enthusiastic, but honestly, I don’t see it in your longer content. For me, those feel like in-depth, educational pieces that deserve full focus and attention. They’re serious, valuable, and always worth the time.

Please take all the time you need — your effort is truly appreciated, and I’m really grateful for all the important new information you continue to share.

video_title: Why Parrots May Bond to One Person (And What You Can Do About It)
video_description: A viewer recently asked me about their Orange Wing Amazon parrot who only allows one person (the owner) to pet or touch him.
text_content:
First off, i wish you were more north so we could grab dinner but wanted to share my piece also i have 5 birds, two green cheeks, two quakers and one lovebird. Both quakers and my second conure were given up and i worked at a bird store at the time (I dont really condone purchasing birds anymore because of actually seeing it, but that's another story). The first conure i brought home was about six months old, now about 3.5 years, its crazy how time passes and she is truly my spirit animal, understands me more than any person. My lovebird never really truly bonded with me but does step up. My green quaker is older at about 10 years old, does not fly, and has only bonded with a family member but it's okay. My blue quaker is pretty sociable with everybody and is definitely the most playful..

And here's my problem child,  my pineapple conure was a rehome and he's about estimated 6/7 years old.. i do not really know his previous situation which i know can complicate things. But he does not like hands at all,  he will hang out on my shoulder and does preen me, never have gotten bitten by him ever. But anybody else, he still does not like hands but he actually really goes to attack anybody else. All this to say i know he does trust me and if i approach very slowly he will let me pet his neck, but very infrequently.. any suggestions on how to work with him to be more acceptable with handling/ hands?

video_title: Why Parrots May Bond to One Person (And What You Can Do About It)
video_description: A viewer recently asked me about their Orange Wing Amazon parrot who only allows one person (the owner) to pet or touch him.
text_content: So what happens if you never touched your bird inappropriately? I brought home a 7 year old, male Quaker Parrot a year ago. From day one he feigned regurgitation for my fingers and tried to mate with my hands.  I hadn't ever scratched him yet on the head let alone anywhere else. I had researched parrots before getting one so I knew not to rub under the wing and such.  I had only ever had him on my shoulder and perched on my finger. I met him once before to see how he liked me. I spent 3 hours with him before coming back a week later to pick him up.  The first time i met him he flew onto my shoulder, stared me down, gave a head bop and proceeded to rub his beak and face into my beard. I thought he was just friendly but now I suspect he was courting me.  Since bringing him home he frequently gets "excited", even if I just talk to him and even just a short amount of head scratches. I had tried various diets, extra sleep. Attempt to discourage the behavior. I never let him mate with my hand and I will pull away, set him down or put him in the cage but nothing has ever worked.

My mum has this uncanny ability to bond with any bird, she's a lot more confident than I am and I think birds can sense my anxiety. I do bond with birds, usually Cockatoos! There was one  in a store, told he'd never talk to me, but I stood by him for a little while and spoke softly, often just saying things like 'Is it true you won't talk to me?' then suddenly he' would waffle/talk to me and the shop keeper, then we started getting along VERY well. More play and bonding, he just wanted to be loved and talked to. The cockatoo was adopted not long after but the keeper thanked me as he remembered how I got along with , apparently the bird was usually very grumpy and quiet before I started visiting. All I know now is he's happy, got a home and a LOT of attention from the new owner. 

My silly question is, do people have a 'type' of bird they bond with or vice versa? x

video_title: Mexican Red Crowned Amazon, The Best Pet Parrot?
video_description: The Red Headed Amazon, aka Green Cheeked Amazon, is an insanely fun big personality parrot but the question is, is this the ...
text_content:
What a lovely, insightful video :)

My grandmother got an Amazon back in the eighties and as you might imagine, back then, no one knew how to take care of such a bird. What little information there was, mostly was aimed to make the owners life easier, not ensure the parrot is kept properly. So, after being caught in the wild as a baby, shipped around the world, he spent his first decade in a way too tiny cage, mostly being ignored, because he wasn't handleable and became more and more aggressive over time.
As soon as I was old enough to realize, I tried and succeeded to bond with him. But being a kid still, I too didn't fully understand how to make his life better. Over time, I learned and tried my best to improve his situation, while he still lived with my granny. One day I finally managed to take him with me.
He now has a huge enclosure and since I work from home, he is with me most of the time. I am glad to be able to keep him near me all the time, since finding a bird friend for him was deemed impossible by several experts - he is absolutely terrified of other birds, probably due to what happened during the transport. Alas, he is enjoying attention and love as much as appropriate food. While I try my very best to make his birdy life an awesome one, we still have to work through a lot of issues due to all the years of neglect. But he is doing a great job and is a wonderful friend and I see that he is happy now.
All that being said, I still feel guilty for not helping him sooner, but I will continue to make it up to him.

Seeing you and your peers doing your best to educate people about those animals is just wonderful and I am sure you are saving many of them from ending up in awful situations. Thank you!

text_content: I've had one for over 30 years.  His name is Gonzo and he is a character.  Occasionally loud at times but not often enough to bother you.  They are smart and will repeat words they hear.  You can teach them a few words.  Gonzo probably has a vocabulary of about 25 different words or sentences that he says.  We had numerous parrots and this is the best one by far.  The others had at least one deal breaker such as too big, too loud, too unruly.  Green cheeks/Red heads are the best overall bird on the planet.

I have a red lorded Amazon. Her name is Ella. She likes being held, but she’s not very cuddly, and she doesn’t like head scratches. She’s 7 years old, and we adopted her at5 years old. We have a open cage rule in our house. We all have a blue & gold macaw that’s also 7, an umbrella cockatoo that’s 24, and a baby 16 weeks old mini severe macaw. 
Ella is ALWAYS into something, swinging on curtains, chewing holes in stuff, and always playing! You can’t talk on the phone around her because she’ll just scream and pretend she’s on the phone 😂 She’s also a big screamer! Usually around 6-7pm she just screams her head off. It does take some getting used to. She’s not nearly as loud as our umbrella cockatoo. Most amazons are pretty happy being only children, and playing alone.

text_content: I have one for 10 years so far.  He is feisty, even though we had him as a baby bird.  I’m the only one that can handle him.  He hates my uniform- he will try to bite hard if I put my hand in his large flight cage wearing my uniform.  He loves listening and copying my hubby, but will bite him if he had the chance.   He calls out my kids names all day if he hasn’t seen them.  He copies a few bird calls, crow, seagull mainly.  He loves to listen to other bird chatters.  Wild parrots have landed on his cage.  He goes on a screeching rampage every morning and evening.  We give him walnuts to try to quiet him at those times.  He bows his head for scratches, he fluffs his feathers when asked to shake, open his wings on command, flaps his wings holding onto perch when I say let’s fly.  He also says rawr, I’m a lion.  You know when he will be mischievous when his eyes pin and he stretches himself out,  and fluffs.  The big give away is the evil laugh-  he definitely goes on the defensive when strangers appear.  I can take him out of the cage and hold him long enough to get him in the house.  He likes to terrorize my hubby unfortunately.  If you wobble your hand, he doesn’t bite and focuses on not falling.  He loves palm fruit, the orange ones.

text_content:
I was once given an older Amazon with a similar beak problem; only worse!  He, too, had been raised on a diet primarily of seed, and his beak was so severely overgrown that my Vet said he couldn't trim it back, because he was afraid his nerves may have been involved. 
So, I provided him with a cuddle bone, rock, stick, and put sandpaper on 1 of the perches in his cage, and also attempted to gradually change his diet to a healthier one (as recommended by my Vet), but he didn't use them, and refused to eat. It was sad. He was a miserable, unhappy boy. Soooooo unlike most parrots that we're used to seeing. 

This video was very informative, and I believe he may have died from liver failure. 

I think a lot of people just sincerely don't know that the bird seed that is sold in Pet Stores would be harmful to their birds.

text_content: Many times I've been bothered by the fact that other friends (and even people) have larger, "more difficult" parrots they keep just fine, without any experience  and tell me they're easy, while I have 1 lovebird and find him challenging.  That doesn't match up. Later on I realized parrots aren't difficult  to own. They're difficult to DO RIGHT and just "owning one" doesn't make a success story. It's be easy for anyone to just own a bird - clean it, feed it... if they don't work with it. My friends African grey is scared of everything so she doesn't do much with him. But an inactive, fearful parrot is not a success even if it doesn't cause issues. So it's important  to note that, yes many owners "cope" but their birds intelligence is unused and unsatisfied. I find my lovebird challenging because I try to keep it mentally stimulated, well-trained, which he is. I can do most things with him my friends can't do with their own parrots.

text_content:
Another good thing to keep in mind is that if you keep a flock, they will help keep each other happy. I have a flock of 4 Bourke's parakeets and they are tame and loving and get plenty of time with me, but they are happy when I'm not around too because they have each other! Remember, birds are social animals. If you just have one bird, you are their flock, and all their social needs must be met by you alone. However, having a flock of multiple birds is not right for everyone, either. Just remember to weigh the bird's needs and your own. If they don't match up, you might not be the best match for that bird.

Your bronze-wing is gorgeous! I absolutely love them. Pionus are so beautiful.

There is one thing that I did want to point out. Your video posting frequency is really low, you post 1 video every three months or so! In my opinion, especially since you aim to review as many parrot species as possible, you should be posting ATLEAST one video per week. That would be more realistic considering your monumental goal. And please, since the large macaws are the most interest inspiring of all parrots PLEASE PLEASE do a video on Blue and Gold and Green Wing macaws. These two are really missing from your video collection. Coupled with your great Scarlet macaw review it would be just awesome to hear about the other two most common species.

Other species I would really recommend you do a video on is the Alexandrine parakeet. They are such an amazing bird native to my part of the world! Also, a video on Major Mitchell would also be great since for many people they are the MOST beautiful of all cockatoo species. Just look at the overall coloration and that crest on them!

I really hope you will respond to this comments. I would love to hear from you. Keep up the good work!

text_content:
Hey there Blue Plant Pets! I just wanted to ask for some help with my bird, since you know a lot about many of them.
I’ve had a few parakeets over the past four years- three total, but sadly two of them died, the last almost a year ago. The parakeet that I have right now is a female yellow-headed, turquoise parakeet, but that isn’t the main problem.
I first got Ari (the parakeet I have right now) along with one other, older parakeet when I was very young. I wasn’t properly trained for these birds, but I at least knew how to feed and water them, and clean their cage. The only thing I wasn’t taught was how to actually take care of them.
The older parakeet had another owner, so she was used to humans. But she died before she could make an impact on Ari. I didn’t properly get her used to me. And although I’ve learned a lot more about taking care of them over the next few years, all the videos or questions or searches have been about first getting birds, not having them for a while.
So, this is the problem I hope you could help me fix. Ari does not let me touch her, let me hold her, or perch on my finger. She most she’ll let me do is hold my finger out for her to gently touch her beak.
Ari has been my friend for years, and I just want to make sure that eventually I’ll be able to show her that I won’t hurt her.

Please and thank you. ❤❤❤

P.S., I am probably going to get a new bird in hopes that if I train the new bird into actually liking me, maybe Ari can learn to like me as well. I don’t know if I will be able to, though.

Hi, I have a male eclectus parrot 🦜 
Their diet is approx 70% sprouted seed and veg to 30% fruit.   My boy Ripper does get pretty sticky at times, but enjoys a shower so it’s easy to deal with.  Of course they are messy, as are all birds.   They are also highly intelligent and very trainable as well as the ability to be fantastic talkers.
They truly are a fantastic companion parrot, but I agree probably not for the first bird.

video_title: Top 5 Popular Parrots to Avoid
video_description: These are super popular parrots BUT they may not be good for beginners except for the absolute bravest of people willing to put ...
text_content:
For beginners I totally understand this list. For anyone with parrot experience, it won’t be that bad. I went from a Green Cheek Conure to a Congo African Grey and our African Grey is honestly easier aside from requiring more toys & general stimulation. She’s never bitten anyone hard enough to break skin, and likes to just hang out most of the time. She’s holds a grudge, but is generally very sweet and loving, even to strangers. 

My conure on the other hand used to attack, constantly. He’d fly for peoples faces and literally rip / bite through skin for seemingly no reason. I’ve honestly had better luck with big birds for temperament than the smaller birds. Though cockatiels are angels in my experience.

video_title: Top 5 Popular Parrots to Avoid
video_description: These are super popular parrots BUT they may not be good for beginners except for the absolute bravest of people willing to put ...
text_content: I have a rescue red sided eclectus that spent a couple of years (before me) locked in a cage in a closet. I've had Pepper about 2 years now, and he's fully feathered again, stopped his toe tapping and is finally relaxed and happy. After I got him, it took him 2-3 weeks to stop biting me just for the hell of it. It took him about 18 months to actually enjoy being touched and cuddled. He's learned to speak a few more things than when he first arrived, but talking is definitely something that is on his terms. A lot of people don't understand that these birds do EVERYTHING on their own terms and at their own pace. Rescuing an eclectus (or any parrot) is one of the most rewarding things, but OMG does it require patience and persistence. My son rescued a 35 year old triton cockatoo. And i absolutely agree with you here. None of these are beginner birds or for the impatient, but they are amazing birds. But really, nothing beats the morning singing of a sweet little cockatiel. text_content: Budgies are actually great talkers and are really smart, most people don’t realize this because they get them at pet stores as adults and they were never raised to be socialized with humans. It’s best to get them from a store or a breeder that specializes in birds when they’re about 6 weeks old because this is when they’re old enough to leave the nest but still young enough to bond with you. If you want your bird to talk, you should get a male. I’ve never successfully got a female budgie to talk but the bond will still be there if you just want a buddy. For best results, you should take your budgies out as much as possible. Only keep them in their cage when they sleep and when you go out, they learn to talk by being around you and the more social they are, the longer and happier their lives will be

text_content:
I had/have three of the parrots on this list. 
My first parrot was a sulphur crested cockatoo, which I got as a kid. She was my beginner bird, & she was not hard to look after. I see cockatoos often mentioned in lists of parrots that are not ideal beginner birds, & yet, mine was the easiest of all my birds to look after. Noise was not an issue. She sometimes squawked in the evenings, but a cocky squawk is not shrill or abrasive like some other parrots. 
I presently have an Amazon & eclectus. The Amazon is not difficult to look after. Even though he’s loud, he’s not noisy. He likes spending time with me but is also happy to chill out on his own. 
I’d agree that eclectus are high maintenance. I wouldn’t recommend one as a first, second or third parrot. Diet is a big deal with mine. I can’t feed him pellets because they make him toe tap. Unlike the eclectus on this list, mine is noisy & wants the most attention. On the positive side, he’s got a sweet personality & has never bitten anyone or me (whereas the Amazon bites). My eclectus has a larger than life personality & loves showing off in front of people. But his excitable nature makes him noisy, & most of his noises are shrill & high pitched.

text_content:
Those are depressingly tiny cages. We really need to stop keeping parrots in cages barely wide enough to stretch their wings or walk more than a few steps. Especially something like macaws need to FLY. Even the reptile world is miles ahead of the bird world in giving their animals enriching, naturalistic enclosures with lots of room.
And I don't know why it is, but in 15+ years, I have never seen a pet palm cockatoo that's not a sickly pale pink. In zoos and in the wild, they have blood red cheeks. There must be something wrong with their diets as pets, and I would be very interested to see the lifespan of pet palms.

(Nitpicky, but hawkheads aren't Amazons in any way. They're from South America, but so are macaws and conures. Hawkheads' closest relatives are caiques.)

video_description: All of Jack's tea ingredients (and their USA alternatives) can be found on my Amazon storefronts: https://linktr.ee/fartbirb My least ...
text_content:
My least favourite social media comment is that I deprive Jack of water so she gulps her warm, parrot-safe tea. And I’m not mad about the people who think this - no one should be angry at them. Animals ARE abused and neglected for social media, and we shouldn’t silence concern - not genuine concern that comes from a place of care for animals. When you silence it, it has this slow and quiet effect and that’s bad.  It helps people get away with stuff that they should be held to account for. But speak up with kindness and concern, rather than assumption and anger. That’s when people are more inclined to listen.

P.S please know that my life is not worth living if I do not boil the kettle, give Jack a spoon to throw AND MAKE THAT BIRD HER TEA. Jack makes most of the rules here and please don’t write any comments asking if Jack is abusing her human because Jack prefers silence over airing those concerns (Jack is the exception to most rules). 

P.s Jack’s vits are mixed into half a mug of cold water, and kettle water then added - stirred and cooled if needed, or if it’s not warm enough for her, more hot added. I always check the temperature before giving her the cup!

text_content: I am so overjoyed that you adopted him with your heart of gold!  You are super special! I have had parrots in my life time, and what upsets me is that people who buy parrots, buy them because they’re cute. But buying any parrot, the buyer must be prepared that the care and demands are high! They require a lot of loving care and a great vet and…money to give them 200% of their patience and quality time.I see on here so many video’s of parrots who people brought back, and it is heartbreaking!  Unlike a dog/cat, whom we love so much,they require a conscientious owner, as they are exotics.  The “return” is a lifetime of joy, laughs, and their precious unconditional love!  He is absolutely adorable! May God bless you, your feathered buddy, and all those in your heart! And I hope your video helps people to understand they are not just an ornament…their God”s very special creation….all my love…👩🏻‍🦳✝🥰❤🙌👑🌹🙏🏻💕☺🦜🦜🦜🥰

text_content: That's how I got my cocktail. He was to loud for my sister and due to her health it got on her nerves. I loved him anyway. I bought my mom a Quaker parrot and he refused to speak in front of me. He eventually slipped up and I finally heard him talk because he didn't know I was in the house. I laughed and I watched him stretch his neck trying to see where I was at. He immediately shut up and didn't say anything until I left the room. He used to go for my feet trying to bite me through my shoes. I asked the woman I got him from about his home life. She had another bird that was a female cockatial so I told my mom he hated me because I took him away from his girlfriend. Such good times. He finally passed and since it was winter and the ground was frozen I wrapped him in a towel, put him in a ziplock bag and placed him in the freezer until we could give him a proper burial. I always wanted another bird but with my two cats I think it would traumatize the bird. I just enjoy my wild birds.

text_content: Sanctuaries are filled with cockatoos. They look fun and snuggly in videos, but they can be unpredictable, aggressive, and LOUD, and I mean hearing loss loud. If there's a falcon within a half mile of my house, everyone within a 2 mile radius will know about it. And that beak is no joke, it can bite through blocks of wood AND your face. So many people don't understand this prior to getting one. That said, they are amazing characters if you commit to changing your life to accommodate them, which includes living with the noise. I love that this woman was volunteering at a parrot rescue when she met this boy. What a great story! And a fantastic way to explore what these birds are really like, and set them up for success.

Cockatoos are long-lived birds, with lifespans typically ranging from 40 to 80 years or even longer in captivity. This is significantly longer than their 20-40 year lifespan in the wild, primarily due to consistent care, protection, and diet in captivity.
Species: Larger species can live even longer, with some documented cases nearing 100 years. For example, the Sulphur-crested Cockatoo can live 70+ years, with potential for over 100 years.

My U2 is not sure what to make of the sounds coming from my iPad right now 😂

My baby came to us back in 2014. We were his 3rd home in 4yrs…because he is loud 😢. He was sooooo afraid of balls because they had been thrown at his cage when he screamed. Afraid of anything moving too fast around him because someone hit his cage….for being a cockatoo and being loud. We love him so much and I cannot imaging having a quiet home…and can never live in an apartment 😂😂😂😂. My oldest…his true human,  will take him when I am too old to care for him. 💖

I am single in large part because when someone walks into my home and my birds (I also have an Amazon) start talking to you and you don’t respond….yeah, that tells me all I need to know. 🤷‍♀️ Sydney has been with my family longer than anyone else in his life and I feel so blessed that he loves us.

Cockatoo breeding should be illegal. They live so long that people have to include their care in a will. One life change causes them to be abandoned, even when loved. text_content:
I have a cat who’s needed 2 kinds of daily medicated eyedrops in one eye for almost a year now. He’s been so easy to train to let me do it without needing to wrap him in a towel or brace him or anything. I just hold his head up and open his eye, drop it, and we’re done. Lots of praise and then treats immediately after. At first I had to hold him a bit more, and he didn’t come to me, I’d go wherever he was, but it wasn’t too long before he started helping. He really likes to help with things, so that’s the language I use. It’s easier because he’s such a sweetheart, but any smarter cat could definitely get here. 

I completely agree that it’s amazing to be be able to do his meds without stressing him or me out! Very much worth the extra effort to get to this point. 😊

video_title: If we can do this, so can you. Restraintless parrot nail trims are doable! #petbirds #rescueparrots
text_content: You're so amazing with birds ❤. I love to see how you work WITH the birds. My parents ended up owning a pet shop back in the late 80's. So I grew up there. My parents really didn't know much about owning a pet shop at the time. We sold small animals & birds but we ended up with so many of them being left on our door step once they weren't so "cute or little" anymore. So we ended up manly selling supplies more than anything. We ended up with so many unwanted parrots & other small animals. We were more a rescue than anything. I loved it of course but it was so sad. So we became a supply store instead and if you wanted to adopt one of the animals you could. I miss those days but they had a hard time keeping the doors open. We had some amazing birds. I LOVE your page ❤.

text_content:
Can you train my dogs? It's been years of work so far (eleven, three, and two and a half respectively) and my dogs are still a battle for nail trimming. My chiweenie has to be muzzled or he will drawn my blood but he's totally fine for the vet and we use the same grinder. 

My not-so-mini aussie tries to pull his paws away for the nail trim but is fine with the scissors and buzzer for his foot hair. He'll lift his leg for me to buzz cut the floof off his "downstairs" so it isn't a trust issue.

My labrashephard pees, a lot, for any number of reasons that I cannot comprehend. I just try to get it over with and they get treats afterwards even when they're awful for it 🤷‍♀️

text_content: I’ve lived nearly 40 years of my life with up to 4 parrots. I discovered a CH conure in a feed store. He was untamed and open banded. It was 1988 and quarantine stations were still legal in California at the time. He had been trying to take baths in the back of the fish tanks. The lady who owned the feed store was a friend and she wanted a good home for him so she gave me a cage and all the supplies for $35 and gave me the bird. She called it a promotion. She just fell in love with him and even though she didn’t want to keep him she knew I would give him the best home. It was a challenge as I had never hand tamed a wild parrot that had never been handled. Luckily there was a robust parrot companion community and I learned how to tame him. My spouse was active duty military at the time and so he was out on a ship at sea in the West Pacific, I had nothing to do in my spare time when I wasn’t at work. My new conure had a pretty portable standard pacific parrot cage. I took him with me whenever I went house sitting or visiting my uncles place in Huntington Beach. So my parrots have always been members of the family and not just exotic pet wallpaper to impress people with. A decades long relationship with a parrot has its ups and downs but as the years go by, the closeness is really priceless. I find the more time we spend together the closer our bond gets. Are there times you get frustrated with them? Of course but I think if you understand how your kind of bird thinks and behavior can be put into that context, you can start to really unlock the key to providing a rewarding life for both you and your birds. I’ve a lot of experience with parrots but I still don’t feel qualified to adopt a cockatoo. They need to be the center of attention and from what I’ve seen especially with Moluccan’s they have a lot of energy. They are so special though just absolutely adorable.

I cannot fathom falling in love with this bird over the course of months of dedication through hard work, (VERY hard work) clear communication, companionship, respect, and training; and then to have to bid him farewell to his new family. 
But, I know without a doubt that Michael the Parrot Wizard would NEVER ever recommend a future family for Cucumber if they weren't thoroughly vetted to the nth degree. And that gives me comfort for when we start to see less and less of Cucumber on this channel. We are all going to miss him so much, but we know for sure he's going to the perfect home that he so much deserves.
And we love that! ❤❤❤❤❤❤❤

video_title: Taylor Swift’s Opalite Music Video Explained | The Hidden Meaning of Her Strangest Love Story
video_description: Grab your emotional support rock. Adjust your scrunchie. Try not to change the channel during the infomercial. In this extended ...
text_content:
Thank you for the generosity of the time and talent that you devoted to this dissection and analysis. I have listened attentively to the podcast, and am enjoying for the second time on YouTube.  My only extra contribution to this masterful summary would be about the care with which Taylor has chosen the metaphors for the exes. Both a rock and cactus have mixed attributes, unlike the garbage that no-one healthy wants to keep around. A rock is how we refer to someone who has been steady and dependable when we’re in times of trouble. They meet immediate needs and they’re so reassuring when we aren’t feeling equipped to deal with present circumstances. Taylor went through a time when a rock was a comfort and a big, safe support. But then she found her feet and her own intrinsic strength again, and her rock couldn’t morph into a more flexible companion, who could meet her emerging and more nuanced needs. But it probably remained a very nice rock. A cactus also has many positive attributes. For instance, they’re often very attractive, and even the dull ones often have gorgeous blooms that suddenly emerge and amaze everyone. They have stores of vital supplies of life-giving water, that can sustain creatures with the skills or correct body types to get past the spikes to reach to that water.  But they aren’t nurturing or sustaining for everyone.  Not everyone can get past those spikes. 
If Taylor wants to make a music video for every song on this album, I will be a very happy fan.

video_title: Taylor Swift’s Opalite Music Video Explained | The Hidden Meaning of Her Strangest Love Story
video_description: Grab your emotional support rock. Adjust your scrunchie. Try not to change the channel during the infomercial. In this extended ...
text_content:
Wow just wow you outdid yourself!
Just a few things I'd like to add or shine another light on. I think that things/scenes/lyrics like the rock and cactus have multiple meanings/origins on purpose because they do not symbolize just one thing/person. I believe she does this in her songs as well and that makes it so relatable as there is something for everyone but also attributable to others (Cowboy like me for Travis or how her previous 6 albums to some are now all about Matty).
1 I think there is more symbolism to the bathtub scene. There was an awful lot of spray on her, how many times did she spray herself trying to get rid of all things that she thought made her the problem while he just calmly cleans her of it.
2 To me in the photograph scene the clothes do not seem to fit Taylor but look more like another set of his. Is she maybe wearing his clothes as she has left her old life behind?
3 There was a tweet/post/tiktok quite a while ago about Travis ex being a diva (in the negative sense) to staff at a restaurant. A waitress said that Travis used to tip them behind her back.
4 When Domhnall beckons her down the stage that step down is again Chiefs colors. From the big stage to a smaller stage to just celebrating each other?
5 Kam is wearing a brooch that looks a lot like the Salvador Dali brooch that he made for Rebekah Harkness.
6 You missed what I thought was the most important part of this whole MV. When they hug after the dance is over you can hear Domhnall saying "You're amazing". That one line to me shows the acceptance and celebration of her in this relation where she is no longer the monster on the hill, at least not for him.

So much info in just 1 hour and 30 minutes about a 6 minute music video that I did not even have time to speed up the playback speed!

video_title: 26 Years Caged: Louie the Macaw’s Rescue Story
video_description: In this heartfelt conversation, we sit down with Kelsey from @NoPlucksGiven to hear the incredible rescue story of Louie, ...
text_content:
I'm currently turning my bed room into her (Aphrodite's/afros) room.
I'm looking for ideas tho.
Ive stock piled enough thick card to build a massive frame for perches climbing...  She will have her own door access to the living room via the top "windows"? Above the UK doors.
Things I've covered, TV, days of macaw videos, kinder eggs for treats and training (hasn't chewed yet)
She isn't a fan of water, but she's got better since she's been at a rescue I volunteer at. She's also eased up on her aggression towards woman, most there are woman, and the treat her... Still working on her aggression tho.
On a plus, our bond is strong, she hated the harness before we got it near her....water,woman and harness...umm what happened in her past?? So I started taking her out at night to see the snow, then across the road to the chippy for " hots" lol.
In all the time she's been outside she's been spooked twice, rained on once and baited as we got to my brothers who she loves, that last time was the only time I had to climb a tree to show her what saws do.... Small branch down, then hers... She saw what I was doing and she fell for it and came back to hand lmao.. 
Yes, she's smart... In trouble? Count 1,2....and she's good.
Tell a joke or use sarcasm? She's first to laugh ???? Tone I'm guessing but ....from wild corvids to a macaw was an amazing step. This spring she should meet squeek, a rook who's partner I helped along with a young female rook. He was put down due to injury but squeek has stayed close ever since, last spring she showed a crow my feeding spots going to work... (robins and jackdaw nesting area)
.
.
Its amazing how many things go on day to day.
Sorry for rambling on

text_content:
I often wonder if parrots' ability to see well into the UV range can make it harder for them to discern between the colors that WE paint and dye things in. Especially with this set, where all of these pieces were presumably painted in the same factory, with paints from the same supplier. What if the paint base, or some other additive is HIGHLY UV reflective? We wouldn't see it, but birds would, and that could make it much harder to discern between these objects.

To be perfectly clear, I am not saying you're doing anything wrong. This is fantastic enrichment if nothing else, and there's certainly a lot of learning/training value here. It is also very cute, and I appreciate you sharing it. I do think it could be worthwhile to check and see what these look like in UV if you haven't already. Just to be sure this isn't a more challenging game than you intend.

video_title: Parrot Who Can't Walk Builds a New Life One Step at a Time
video_description: This story follows Gina, a parrot whose feet were badly broken by a human and healed the wrong way. Unable to perch or walk ...
text_content:
It really angers me knowing Gina's toes and foot bones were broken, and then _she was left to suffer that way._ 

We always hope that only decent, caring humans keep other species as companions, but then we are confronted with the ugly truth. 

Divine intervention occurred when Michele found out about Gina and did everything in her power to bring Gina home with her. 

And Gina knew this, too. Birds can perceive differences between humans, and Gina realized that trustworthy and loving humans _do_ exist. 

It's why she is recovering so well, considering the extent of  permanent disability and disfigurement she was left with. Which was on purpose, it appears.

Having adopted a smaller parrot who went through the same kind of physical abuse and who receiving no treatment and care, I also sought veterinary help for him after freeing him from his captors (Little Star). 

A twisted, hobbling little bird who was the size of a 3-month old instead of an adult, the veterinarian who examined him was holding back tears on realizing someone did to Little Star what had been done to Gina. There was no way to repair the damage once the bones had knit. 

He devised a rehabilitation plan for me to work with Little Star, who also "walked on his legs" instead of his feet. 

These precious birds don't deserve to suffer, let alone purposefully. 

In the end, for however long Gina's life will be, at least she will be safe, loved, given the medical and flock care she needs and deserves, all thanks to a _good_ lady with a humble soul, a big heart, and common sense.

_Thank goodness, thank goodness, thank goodness._

🦜 💔 😢  ----> 👩 ❤ 🦜 😊
